**Miami**[[b]](#cite_note-12) is a [coastal](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") city in the U.S. state of [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida"). It is the [second-most populous city proper](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Florida "List of municipalities in Florida") in Florida, with a population of 442,241 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census").[[5]](#cite_note-2020CensusPopulationByRace-6) The [Miami metropolitan area](/wiki/Miami_metropolitan_area "Miami metropolitan area") in [South Florida](/wiki/South_Florida "South Florida") has an estimated 6.39 million residents, ranking as the fifth-largest metropolitan area in the [Southeast](/wiki/Southeastern_United_States "Southeastern United States") and [eighth-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area#United_States "Metropolitan statistical area") in the United States.[[7]](#cite_note-2020Pop-8) Miami has the [third-largest skyline](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_the_United_States#Cities_with_the_most_skyscrapers "List of tallest buildings in the United States") in the U.S. with over [300 high-rises](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Miami "List of tallest buildings in Miami"),[[11]](#cite_note-13) 70 of which exceed 491 ft (150 m).[[12]](#cite_note-14) It is the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Miami-Dade County](/wiki/Miami-Dade_County,_Florida "Miami-Dade County, Florida").

Miami is a major center and leader in finance, commerce, culture, arts, and international trade.[[13]](#cite_note-GAWC-15)[[14]](#cite_note-16) Miami's metropolitan area is by far the largest urban economy in Florida, with a 2023 [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") of $533.674 billion.[[9]](#cite_note-GDP-10) In a 2018 [UBS](/wiki/UBS "UBS") study of 77 world cities, Miami was the third-richest city in the U.S. and the third-richest globally in [purchasing power](/wiki/Purchasing_power "Purchasing power").[[15]](#cite_note-17) Miami is a [majority-minority city](/wiki/Majority_minority_in_the_United_States "Majority minority in the United States"), with 70.2 percent of the city's population identifying as [Hispanic and Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") as of 2020.[[16]](#cite_note-2020Hispanic-18)

[Downtown Miami](/wiki/Greater_Downtown_Miami "Greater Downtown Miami") has among the largest concentrations of international banks in the U.S. and is home to several large national and international companies.[[17]](#cite_note-19)[[18]](#cite_note-20) The [Health District](/wiki/Health_District_(Miami) "Health District (Miami)") is home to several major [University of Miami](/wiki/University_of_Miami "University of Miami")-affiliated hospital and health facilities, including [Jackson Memorial Hospital](/wiki/Jackson_Memorial_Hospital "Jackson Memorial Hospital"), the nation's largest hospital with 1,547 beds,[[19]](#cite_note-21) and the [Miller School of Medicine](/wiki/Miller_School_of_Medicine "Miller School of Medicine"), the University of Miami's academic medical center and teaching hospital, and others engaged in health-related care and research. [PortMiami](/wiki/PortMiami "PortMiami"), the city's seaport, is the busiest [cruise](/wiki/Cruise_ship "Cruise ship") port in the world in both passenger traffic and cruise lines.[[20]](#cite_note-22)

The Miami metropolitan area is the second-most visited city or metropolitan statistical area in the U.S. after [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), with over four million visitors in 2022.[[21]](#cite_note-23) Due to its strong commercial and cultural ties to [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") and majority-[Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") population (at 70%), Miami has been called the "Gateway to Latin America" or even the "Capital of Latin America".[[22]](#cite_note-24)[[23]](#cite_note-25)[[24]](#cite_note-26)

## Toponymy

Miami was named after the [Miami River](/wiki/Miami_River_(Florida) "Miami River (Florida)"), derived from [Mayaimi](/wiki/Mayaimi "Mayaimi"), the historic name of [Lake Okeechobee](/wiki/Lake_Okeechobee "Lake Okeechobee") and the Native Americans who lived around it.[[25]](#cite_note-27) Miami is sometimes colloquially referred to as *The [305](/wiki/Area_codes_305,_786,_and_645 "Area codes 305, 786, and 645")*, *Magic City*, *Gateway to the Americas*, *Gateway to Latin America*, *Capital of Latin America*,[[1]](#cite_note-Time-1) and *Vice City*.

## History

Main article: [History of Miami](/wiki/History_of_Miami "History of Miami")

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Miami](/wiki/Timeline_of_Miami "Timeline of Miami").

See also: [Cuban migration to Miami](/wiki/Cuban_migration_to_Miami "Cuban migration to Miami") and [National Register of Historic Places listings in Miami](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_Miami "National Register of Historic Places listings in Miami")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/MiamiAvenue1896.jpg/250px-MiamiAvenue1896.jpg)](/wiki/File:MiamiAvenue1896.jpg)

In 1896, approximately 400 men gathered in the building pictured on the left and voted to incorporate Miami.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Mouth_of_Miami_River_20100211.jpg/250px-Mouth_of_Miami_River_20100211.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mouth_of_Miami_River_20100211.jpg)

The mouth of [Miami River](/wiki/Miami_River_(Florida) "Miami River (Florida)") at [Brickell Key](/wiki/Brickell_Key "Brickell Key") in February 2010

The [Tequesta](/wiki/Tequesta "Tequesta") tribe occupied the Miami area for around 2,000 years before contact with Europeans. A village of hundreds of people, dating to 500–600 BCE, was located at the mouth of the [Miami River](/wiki/Miami_River_(Florida) "Miami River (Florida)"). It is believed that the entire tribe migrated to [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba") by the mid-1700s.[[26]](#cite_note-28) In 1566, admiral [Pedro Menéndez de Avilés](/wiki/Pedro_Men%C3%A9ndez_de_Avil%C3%A9s "Pedro Menéndez de Avilés"), Florida's first governor, claimed the area for [Spain](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire"). A Spanish [mission](/wiki/Mission_(station) "Mission (station)") was constructed one year later. Florida was ruled by Spain for centuries—aside from a brief British interlude (1763–1783) that ended with [Britain](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") losing the territory back to Spain—until Spain ceded it to the United States in 1821, in exchange for U.S. recognition of Spanish sovereignty in [Texas](/wiki/Spanish_Texas "Spanish Texas") and the resolution of the border dispute along the [Sabine River](/wiki/Sabine_River_(Texas%E2%80%93Louisiana) "Sabine River (Texas–Louisiana)"). In 1836, the U.S. built [Fort Dallas](/wiki/Fort_Dallas "Fort Dallas") on the banks of the Miami River as part of their development of the [Florida Territory](/wiki/Florida_Territory "Florida Territory") and their attempt to suppress and remove the [Seminoles](/wiki/Seminole "Seminole"). As a result, the Miami area became a site of fighting in the [Second Seminole War](/wiki/Second_Seminole_War "Second Seminole War").

Miami is noted as the only major city in the United States founded by a woman. [Julia Tuttle](/wiki/Julia_Tuttle "Julia Tuttle"), a local [citrus grower](/wiki/Citrus "Citrus") and a wealthy [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland") native, was the original owner of the land upon which Miami was built.[[27]](#cite_note-29) In the late 19th century, the area was known as "Biscayne Bay Country", and reports described it as a promising wilderness and "one of the finest building sites in Florida".[[28]](#cite_note-30)[[29]](#cite_note-31) The [Great Freeze](/wiki/Great_Freeze "Great Freeze") of 1894–1895 hastened Miami's growth, as the crops there were the only ones in Florida that survived. Julia Tuttle subsequently convinced [railroad tycoon](/wiki/Business_magnate "Business magnate") [Henry Flagler](/wiki/Henry_Flagler "Henry Flagler") to extend his [Florida East Coast Railway](/wiki/Florida_East_Coast_Railway "Florida East Coast Railway") to the region, for which she became known as "the mother of Miami".[[30]](#cite_note-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33) Miami was officially incorporated as a city on July 28, 1896, with a population of just over 300.[[32]](#cite_note-34)

In the early twentieth century, Miami's development was deeply shaped by waves of migrant labor, particularly from the Caribbean. Scholars note that Bahamian immigrants formed a critical portion of Miami's construction and service workforce during the city's formative decades, contributing essential labor to roadbuilding, hotel construction, and other urban expansion projects (Shell-Weiss, 2005). Their role in Miami's labor force helped anchor the city's early economic growth and laid a foundation for its later multicultural identity.[[33]](#cite_note-35) During the early 20th century, migrants from the [Bahamas](/wiki/The_Bahamas "The Bahamas") and African-Americans constituted 40 percent of the city's population.[[34]](#cite_note-Concrete-36): 25 When landlords began to rent homes to African-Americans around Avenue J, what would later become NW Fifth Avenue, a gang of white men with torches marched through the neighborhood and warned the residents to move or be bombed.[[34]](#cite_note-Concrete-36): 33 Miami's economy was heavily based on seasonal tourism and construction work, which created a volatile job market of low-wage, temporary employment and contributed to pronounced income inequality even as the city promoted itself as a booming leisure destination.[[35]](#cite_note-37)

Despite the city's image of resort-style prosperity, workers and activists during the interwar period organized around unemployment and labor exclusion, helping to lay early foundations for labor mobilization in South Florida.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[36]](#cite_note-38)

Miami prospered during the 1920s with an increase in population and development in infrastructure as northerners moved to the city. The legacy of [Jim Crow](/wiki/Jim_Crow_laws "Jim Crow laws") was embedded in these developments. Miami's chief of police at the time, [H. Leslie Quigg](/wiki/H._Leslie_Quigg "H. Leslie Quigg"), did not hide the fact that he, like many other white Miami police officers, was a member of the [Ku Klux Klan](/wiki/Ku_Klux_Klan "Ku Klux Klan"). Unsurprisingly, these officers enforced social codes far beyond the written law. Quigg, for example, "personally and publicly beat a colored bellboy to death for speaking directly to a white woman".[[34]](#cite_note-Concrete-36): 53[[37]](#cite_note-39) The collapse of the [Florida land boom of the 1920s](/wiki/Florida_land_boom_of_the_1920s "Florida land boom of the 1920s"), the [1926 Miami Hurricane](/wiki/1926_Miami_Hurricane "1926 Miami Hurricane"), and the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") in the 1930s slowed development. When [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") began, Miami became a base for U.S. defense against [German submarines](/wiki/U-boat "U-boat") due to its prime location on the southern coast of Florida. This brought an increase in Miami's population; 172,172 people lived in the city by 1940. The [city's nickname](/wiki/Nicknames_of_Miami "Nicknames of Miami"), *The Magic City*, came from its rapid growth, which was noticed by winter visitors who remarked that the city grew so much from one year to the next that it was like magic.[[38]](#cite_note-MDInfocenter-40)

After [Fidel Castro](/wiki/Fidel_Castro "Fidel Castro") rose to power in [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba") following the [Revolution](/wiki/Cuban_Revolution "Cuban Revolution") in 1959, many wealthy Cubans sought refuge in Miami, further increasing the city's population. Miami's national profile expanded dramatically in the 1970s, particularly in 1972.[[39]](#cite_note-SFMN_1972-41) The region hosted both the [Democratic](/wiki/1972_Democratic_National_Convention "1972 Democratic National Convention") and [Republican National Conventions](/wiki/1972_Republican_National_Convention "1972 Republican National Convention") in the [1972 Presidential election](/wiki/1972_United_States_presidential_election "1972 United States presidential election"). The [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins") also made history with their [undefeated "perfect" season](/wiki/1972_Miami_Dolphins_season "1972 Miami Dolphins season"). The area's educational and cultural institutions also developed significantly in this period, positioning the city to service a larger and increasingly international population.[[39]](#cite_note-SFMN_1972-41)

Miami developed new businesses and cultural amenities as part of the [New South](/wiki/New_South "New South") in the 1980s and 1990s. At the same time, South Florida weathered social problems related to [drug wars](/wiki/War_on_drugs "War on drugs"), immigration from [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti") and Latin America, and the widespread destruction of [Hurricane Andrew](/wiki/Hurricane_Andrew "Hurricane Andrew").[[40]](#cite_note-42)[[38]](#cite_note-MDInfocenter-40) Racial and cultural tensions sometimes sparked, but Miami developed in the latter half of the 20th century as a major international, financial, and cultural center. It is the second-largest U.S. city with a Spanish-speaking majority (after [El Paso, Texas](/wiki/El_Paso,_Texas "El Paso, Texas")), and the largest city with a [Cuban-American](/wiki/Cuban-American "Cuban-American") plurality.[[41]](#cite_note-43)[[42]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-44)

## Geography

Miami and its suburbs are located on a broad plain between the [Everglades](/wiki/Everglades "Everglades") to the west and [Biscayne Bay](/wiki/Biscayne_Bay "Biscayne Bay") to the east, which extends from [Lake Okeechobee](/wiki/Lake_Okeechobee "Lake Okeechobee") southward to [Florida Bay](/wiki/Florida_Bay "Florida Bay"). The elevation of the area averages at around 6 ft (1.8 m)[[43]](#cite_note-45) above sea level in most neighborhoods, especially near the coast. The highest points are found along the [Miami Rock Ridge](/wiki/Miami_Rock_Ridge "Miami Rock Ridge"), which lies under most of the eastern Miami metro. The main portion of Miami is on the shores of Biscayne Bay, which contains several hundred natural and artificial [barrier islands](/wiki/Barrier_island "Barrier island"), the largest of which contains [Miami Beach](/wiki/Miami_Beach,_Florida "Miami Beach, Florida") and [South Beach](/wiki/South_Beach "South Beach"). The [Gulf Stream](/wiki/Gulf_Stream "Gulf Stream"), a warm [ocean current](/wiki/Ocean_current "Ocean current"), runs northward just 15 miles (24 km) off the coast, allowing Miami's climate to stay warm and mild all year.

### Geology

The surface bedrock under the Miami area is called [Miami oolite](/wiki/Miami_oolite "Miami oolite") or Miami limestone. This bedrock is covered by a thin layer of soil, and is no more than 50 feet (15 m) thick. Miami limestone formed as the result of the drastic changes in sea level associated with recent [glacial periods](/wiki/Glacial_period "Glacial period"), or [ice ages](/wiki/Ice_age "Ice age"). Beginning some 130,000 years ago, the [Sangamonian Stage](/wiki/Sangamonian "Sangamonian") raised sea levels to approximately 25 feet (8 m) above the current level. All of southern Florida was covered by a shallow sea. Several parallel lines of reef formed along the edge of the submerged Florida [plateau](/wiki/Plateau "Plateau"), stretching from the present Miami area to what is now the [Dry Tortugas](/wiki/Dry_Tortugas "Dry Tortugas").[[44]](#cite_note-geology-46)

The area behind this reef line was a large lagoon. Miami limestone formed throughout the area from the deposition of oolites and the shells of [bryozoans](/wiki/Bryozoans "Bryozoans"). Starting about 100,000 years ago, the [Wisconsin glaciation](/wiki/Wisconsin_glaciation "Wisconsin glaciation") began lowering sea levels, exposing the floor of the lagoon. By 15,000 years ago, the sea level had dropped 300 to 350 feet (90 to 110 m) below the current level. The sea level rose quickly after that, stabilizing at the current level about 4,000 years ago, leaving the [mainland](/wiki/Mainland "Mainland") of South Florida just [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level").[[44]](#cite_note-geology-46)

Beneath the plain lies the [Biscayne Aquifer](/wiki/Biscayne_Aquifer "Biscayne Aquifer"), a natural underground source of fresh water that extends from southern [Palm Beach County](/wiki/Palm_Beach_County,_Florida "Palm Beach County, Florida") to [Florida Bay](/wiki/Florida_Bay "Florida Bay"). It comes closest to the surface around the cities of [Miami Springs](/wiki/Miami_Springs,_Florida "Miami Springs, Florida") and [Hialeah](/wiki/Hialeah,_Florida "Hialeah, Florida").[[45]](#cite_note-47) Most of the [Miami metropolitan area](/wiki/Miami_metropolitan_area "Miami metropolitan area") obtains its drinking water from the Biscayne Aquifer. As a result of the aquifer, it is not possible to dig more than 15 to 20 ft (5 to 6 m) beneath the city without hitting water, which impedes underground construction, though some [underground parking garages](/wiki/Underground_parking_in_South_Florida "Underground parking in South Florida") exist. For this reason, the [mass transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") systems in and around Miami are elevated or [at-grade](/wiki/Land_grading "Land grading").[[44]](#cite_note-geology-46)

Most of the western fringes of Miami border the [Everglades](/wiki/Everglades "Everglades"), a tropical marshland covering most of the southern portion of Florida. [Alligators](/wiki/American_alligator "American alligator") that live in the marshes have ventured into Miami communities and onto major highways.[[44]](#cite_note-geology-46)

#### Neighborhoods

Main article: [Neighborhoods in Miami](/wiki/Neighborhoods_in_Miami "Neighborhoods in Miami")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Miami_neighborhoodsmap.png/250px-Miami_neighborhoodsmap.png)](/wiki/File:Miami_neighborhoodsmap.png)

Map of Miami neighborhoods

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Miamihighpoint.jpg/250px-Miamihighpoint.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miamihighpoint.jpg)

A view from one of the higher points in Miami, west of [Downtown Miami](/wiki/Greater_Downtown_Miami "Greater Downtown Miami"). The highest natural point in Miami is in [Coconut Grove](/wiki/Coconut_Grove "Coconut Grove"), near [Biscayne Bay](/wiki/Biscayne_Bay "Biscayne Bay") along the [Miami Rock Ridge](/wiki/Miami_Rock_Ridge "Miami Rock Ridge") at 24 feet (7.3 m) [above sea level](/wiki/Meters_above_sea_level "Meters above sea level").[[46]](#cite_note-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Old_U.S._Post_Office_and_Courthouse_%28Miami%2C_Florida%29.jpg/250px-Old_U.S._Post_Office_and_Courthouse_%28Miami%2C_Florida%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_U.S._Post_Office_and_Courthouse_(Miami,_Florida).jpg)

The historic district of [Downtown Miami](/wiki/Greater_Downtown_Miami "Greater Downtown Miami") is one of the city's oldest with buildings constructed as far back as 1896.

Miami is split roughly into north, south, west, and Downtown areas. The heart of the city is [Downtown Miami](/wiki/Greater_Downtown_Miami "Greater Downtown Miami"), which is on the eastern side and includes the neighborhoods of [Brickell](/wiki/Brickell "Brickell"), [Virginia Key](/wiki/Virginia_Key "Virginia Key"), [Watson Island](/wiki/Watson_Island "Watson Island"), and [PortMiami](/wiki/PortMiami "PortMiami"). Downtown Miami is Florida's largest and most influential central business district, with many major banks, courthouses, financial headquarters, cultural and tourist attractions, schools, parks, and a large residential population. [Brickell Avenue](/wiki/Brickell_Avenue "Brickell Avenue") has the largest concentration of international banks in the United States. Just northwest of Downtown is the [Health District](/wiki/Health_District_(Miami) "Health District (Miami)"), which is Miami's center for hospitals, [research institutes](/wiki/Research_institute "Research institute") and [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"), with hospitals such as [Jackson Memorial Hospital](/wiki/Jackson_Memorial_Hospital "Jackson Memorial Hospital"), [Bascom Palmer Eye Institute](/wiki/Bascom_Palmer_Eye_Institute "Bascom Palmer Eye Institute"), and the [University of Miami](/wiki/University_of_Miami "University of Miami")'s [Miller School of Medicine](/wiki/Miller_School_of_Medicine "Miller School of Medicine").[[47]](#cite_note-neighborhoods-49)

The southern side of Miami includes the neighborhoods of [Coral Way](/wiki/Coral_Way "Coral Way"), [The Roads](/wiki/The_Roads "The Roads"), and [Coconut Grove](/wiki/Coconut_Grove "Coconut Grove"). Coral Way is a historic residential neighborhood built in 1922 between Downtown and [Coral Gables](/wiki/Coral_Gables,_Florida "Coral Gables, Florida"), and is home to many old homes and tree-lined streets. Coconut Grove, settled in 1825, and annexed into Miami in 1925, is a historic neighborhood with narrow, winding roads and a heavy tree canopy.[[47]](#cite_note-neighborhoods-49)[[48]](#cite_note-50) It is the location of Miami's City Hall at [Dinner Key](/wiki/Dinner_Key "Dinner Key"), the former [Coconut Grove Playhouse](/wiki/Coconut_Grove_Playhouse "Coconut Grove Playhouse"), [CocoWalk](/wiki/CocoWalk "CocoWalk"), and the [Coconut Grove Convention Center](/wiki/Coconut_Grove_Convention_Center "Coconut Grove Convention Center"). It is home to many [nightclubs](/wiki/Nightclub "Nightclub"), bars, restaurants, and [bohemian](/wiki/Bohemianism "Bohemianism") shops, which makes it very popular with local [college students](/wiki/College_student "College student"). Coconut Grove is known for its many parks and gardens, such as [Vizcaya Museum](/wiki/Vizcaya_Museum_and_Gardens "Vizcaya Museum and Gardens"), [The Kampong](/wiki/The_Kampong "The Kampong"), [The Barnacle Historic State Park](/wiki/The_Barnacle_Historic_State_Park "The Barnacle Historic State Park"), and numerous other historic homes and estates.[[47]](#cite_note-neighborhoods-49)

The western side of Miami includes the neighborhoods of [Little Havana](/wiki/Little_Havana "Little Havana"), [West Flagler](/wiki/West_Flagler "West Flagler"), and [Flagami](/wiki/Flagami "Flagami"). Although at one time a mostly Jewish neighborhood, today western Miami is home to immigrants from mostly Central America and [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"). The west central neighborhood of [Allapattah](/wiki/Allapattah "Allapattah") is a multicultural community of many ethnicities.[[47]](#cite_note-neighborhoods-49)

The northern side of Miami includes [Midtown](/wiki/Midtown_Miami "Midtown Miami"), a district with a great mix of diversity ranging from Caribbeans to Central Americans, South Americans and Europeans. The [Edgewater](/wiki/Edgewater_(Miami) "Edgewater (Miami)") neighborhood of Midtown is mostly composed of high-rise residential towers and is home to the [Adrienne Arsht Center for the Performing Arts](/wiki/Adrienne_Arsht_Center_for_the_Performing_Arts "Adrienne Arsht Center for the Performing Arts"). [Wynwood](/wiki/Wynwood_Art_District "Wynwood Art District") is an art district with ten galleries in former warehouses, as well as a large outdoor mural project. The wealthier residents of Miami usually live in the [Design District](/wiki/Miami_Design_District "Miami Design District") and the [Upper Eastside](/wiki/Upper_Eastside "Upper Eastside"), which has many 1920s homes as well as examples of [Miami Modern architecture](/wiki/Miami_Modern_architecture "Miami Modern architecture") in the MiMo Historic District.[[49]](#cite_note-51) The northern side of Miami also has notable African-American and [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") immigrant communities, including [Little Haiti](/wiki/Little_Haiti "Little Haiti"), [Overtown](/wiki/Overtown_(Miami) "Overtown (Miami)") (home of the [Lyric Theater](/wiki/Lyric_Theater_(Miami) "Lyric Theater (Miami)")), and [Liberty City](/wiki/Liberty_City_(Miami) "Liberty City (Miami)").[[47]](#cite_note-neighborhoods-49)

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of Miami](/wiki/Climate_of_Miami "Climate of Miami")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Miamisummershower.png/250px-Miamisummershower.png)](/wiki/File:Miamisummershower.png)

A summer afternoon thunderstorm rolling into Miami from the [Everglades](/wiki/Everglades "Everglades"), July 2006

Miami has a [tropical monsoon climate](/wiki/Tropical_monsoon_climate "Tropical monsoon climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Am*)[[50]](#cite_note-Miami,_Florida-52)[[51]](#cite_note-53) with hot and wet summers and warm and dry winters.

Miami's sea-level elevation, coastal location, position just above the [Tropic of Cancer](/wiki/Tropic_of_Cancer "Tropic of Cancer"), and proximity to the [Gulf Stream](/wiki/Gulf_Stream "Gulf Stream") shape its climate. Average winter high temperatures, from December to March, range from 76.4–80.3 °F (24.7–26.8 °C). January is the coolest month with an average daily temperature of 68.2 °F (20.1 °C). Low temperatures fall below 50 °F (10 °C) about 3 to 4 nights during the winter season,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] after the passage of [cold fronts](/wiki/Cold_front "Cold front") that produce what little rainfall that falls in the winter.

There are two basic seasons in Miami, a hot and wet season from May to October, and a warm and dry season from November to April. During the hot and wet season, daily thundershowers occur in the humid unstable air masses. The wet season in Miami is defined as the period during which the average daily [dew point](/wiki/Dew_point "Dew point") temperature is above 70 °F (21 °C). The rainy season typically begins on the first day that occurs, or within a few days later.[[52]](#cite_note-weather.gov-54)

Daily rainfall in Miami decreases sharply when the average daily dew point falls to 70 °F (21 °C) or below. In some years, a stalled front to the south of the Florida peninsula may cause rains to continue for a few more days. From 1956 to 1997, the date summer began ranged from April 16 to June 3, with a median date of May 21. In those same years, the date summer ended ranged from September 24 to November 1, with a median date of October 17.[[52]](#cite_note-weather.gov-54)

During summer, temperatures range from the mid-80s to low 90s °F (29–35 °C) and are accompanied by high humidity. The heat is often relieved in the afternoon by thunderstorms or a [sea breeze](/wiki/Sea_breeze "Sea breeze") that develops off the Atlantic Ocean. Much of the year's 61.9 inches (1,572 mm) of rainfall occurs during this period. [Dew points](/wiki/Dew_point "Dew point") in the warm months range from 71.9 °F (22.2 °C) in June to 73.7 °F (23.2 °C) in August.[[53]](#cite_note-NOAA-55)

Historical temperature extremes range from 27 °F (−2.8 °C) on February 3, 1917, to 100 °F (38 °C) on July 21, 1942.[[54]](#cite_note-56) While Miami has never [recorded snowfall](/wiki/Snow_in_Florida "Snow in Florida") at any official weather station since records have been kept, [snow flurries](/wiki/Snow_flurry "Snow flurry") fell in some parts of Miami on January 19, 1977.[[55]](#cite_note-57)[[56]](#cite_note-58)[[57]](#cite_note-59) The coldest daytime maximum temperature on record is 45 °F (7 °C) in December 1989 during the [December 1989 United States cold wave](/wiki/December_1989_United_States_cold_wave "December 1989 United States cold wave"). The warmest overnight low measured is 84 °F (29 °C) on several occasions.[[53]](#cite_note-NOAA-55)

[Hurricane season](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone "Tropical cyclone") officially runs from June 1 to November 30, although hurricanes can develop beyond those dates. The most likely time for Miami to be hit is during the peak of the [Cape Verde](/wiki/Cape_Verde_hurricane "Cape Verde hurricane") season, which is mid-August to the end of September.[[58]](#cite_note-60) Although tornadoes are uncommon in the area, one struck in [1925](/wiki/1925_Miami_tornado "1925 Miami tornado") and another in [1997](/wiki/1997_Miami_tornado "1997 Miami tornado").[[59]](#cite_note-61)[[60]](#cite_note-62) Around 40% of homes in Miami are built upon [floodplains](/wiki/Floodplain "Floodplain") and are considered as flood-risk zones.[[61]](#cite_note-63)

Miami falls within the [Department of Agriculture's](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Agriculture "United States Department of Agriculture") 10b/11a plant [hardiness zone](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone").[[62]](#cite_note-64)

Miami is one of the major coastal cities and major cities in the United States that will be most affected by [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change").[[63]](#cite_note-65)[[64]](#cite_note-66) Globally, it is one of the most at-risk cities, according to a 2020 report by [Resources for the Future](/wiki/Resources_for_the_Future "Resources for the Future").[[65]](#cite_note-67)[[66]](#cite_note-:1-68) Global [sea level rise](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise"). Historical tide-gauge observations show a measurable upward trend in sea level along Florida's coastline, and projections estimate that Miami may experience significantly higher flooding frequency by mid-century [[67]](#cite_note-69) which in Miami is projected to be 21 inches (53 cm) to 40 inches (100 cm) by 2070, will lead to an increase in storm damage, more intense flooding, and will threaten Miami's water supply.[[68]](#cite_note-70)[[69]](#cite_note-71)[[70]](#cite_note-72) Other potential impacts of climate change include higher hurricane wind speeds and severe thunderstorms, which can bring about hail or tornadoes.[[66]](#cite_note-:1-68) Some protective efforts are in place, including nourishing beaches and adding protective barriers, raising buildings and roads that are vulnerable, and restoring natural habitats such as [wetlands](/wiki/Wetland "Wetland").[[66]](#cite_note-:1-68) Miami Beach has invested $500 million to protect roads, buildings, and water systems.[[66]](#cite_note-:1-68) Real estate prices in Miami already reflect the increase in prices for real estate at a higher elevation within the city compared to real estate at a lower elevation.[[71]](#cite_note-73)

Miami is the largest city in [South Florida](/wiki/South_Florida "South Florida"), the second-largest city in [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida"), and is the anchor of the largest metropolitan area in Florida: the [Miami metropolitan area](/wiki/Miami_metropolitan_area "Miami metropolitan area"), which has over 6 million residents. Despite Miami being home to less than a fourteenth (1/14) of the population of the metro area, it is an outlier compared to its neighbors, being nearly twice the size of the next-largest city in the metro: [Hialeah](/wiki/Hialeah "Hialeah"). Miami has approximately a sixth of the population of its own county, [Miami-Dade](/wiki/Miami-Dade_County "Miami-Dade County"), which is the state's largest.

Miami had rapid growth in the first half of the twentieth century. Its population grew from 1,681 in the [1900 census](/wiki/1900_United_States_census "1900 United States census") to 249,276 in the [1950 census](/wiki/1950_United_States_census "1950 United States census"). This made it Florida's largest city, a title it retained until the [Jacksonville Consolidation](/wiki/Jacksonville_Consolidation "Jacksonville Consolidation"), when the city of Jacksonville absorbed most of [Duval County](/wiki/Duval_County,_Florida "Duval County, Florida"), nearly tripling its population. Since then, Miami has retained its spot as Florida's second-largest city.

Throughout the latter half of the twentieth century, Miami experienced a certain amount of stagnation in its population, with expansion slowing during the 1950s and 1960s before nearly halting in the next three decades as suburbanization occurred. Miami grew by 34.3% in the 1950s and 1960s as its population reached 334,859 at the [1970 census](/wiki/1970_United_States_census "1970 United States census"). In the next three decades, it only grew 8.2%. By the time of the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census"), Miami's population stood at 362,470.

In the 2000s and 2010s, spurred by high-rise construction in Downtown Miami, Edgewater, and Brickell, Miami's population began to grow quickly once more.[[83]](#cite_note-DowntownGrowth1-87) An estimate by the American Community Survey found that the downtown population, from Brickell north to Midtown Miami, grew nearly 40% between 2010 and 2018.[[84]](#cite_note-DowntownGrowth2-88) From 2000 to 2010, Miami's population grew by 10.2% and reached 399,457 in 2010. In the early 2010s, Miami's population crossed a milestone of 400,000 people. In the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), it had grown by a further 10.7%, up to a population of 442,241.

In 1970, the Census Bureau reported Miami's population as 45.3% Hispanic, 32.9% non-Hispanic White, and 22.7% Black.[[86]](#cite_note-90) Miami's explosive population growth has been driven by internal migration from other parts of the country, up until the 1960s. From 1970 to 2000, population growth in Miami was stagnant, as [non-Hispanic White](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_White "Non-Hispanic White") Miamians left and significant immigration from Latin America, particularly [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"), made up the balance.[[87]](#cite_note-91)[[88]](#cite_note-92) Miami's Hispanic majority solidified itself in this period of time, and in 1985, Miami elected its first Cuban-born mayor, [Xavier Suarez](/wiki/Xavier_Suarez "Xavier Suarez").

According to the U.S. Census Bureau's 2024 population estimates, Miami-dade county continues to experience steady growth, driven largely by domestic migration and ongoing international immigration. The county's population in 2024 reflects its status as one of the most diverse metropolitan regions in the United States, with large Hispanic, Caribbean, and Latin American communities contributing to its demographic profile (U.S Census Bureau, 2024)[[89]](#cite_note-93)

The non-Hispanic Black population of the city of Miami peaked in 1990 at almost 90,000, making up nearly a quarter of the population of Miami. Since then, Miami's non-Hispanic Black population has experienced a precipitous and steady decline. In the 2020 census, it was 52,447, only 11.7% of the population. Reasons for this include high costs in areas such as [Liberty City](/wiki/Liberty_City,_Miami "Liberty City, Miami") and [Little Haiti](/wiki/Little_Haiti "Little Haiti"), compounded with [gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification").[[90]](#cite_note-94)[[91]](#cite_note-95)

The non-Hispanic White population began to rebound in the twenty-first century, as the monolithically Hispanic areas in the Western and Central parts of Miami experienced population stagnation. This caused them to begin to be outweighed by migration into the Downtown region, from Latin America and the rest of the United States. This caused the non-Hispanic White population to rise from a nadir of 11.8% at the time of the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census") to 11.9% at the time of the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"). After this, the non-Hispanic White population grew significantly faster than Miami as a whole did during the 2010s. In the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), non-Hispanic Whites were 14.0% of the population of Miami and numbered 61,829, the highest number since the 1980s. The non-Hispanic White population of Miami surpassed the non-Hispanic Black population of Miami in the 2010s.

In 2010, 34.4% of city residents were of [Cuban](/wiki/Cuban_people "Cuban people") origin, 15.8% had a [Central American](/wiki/Central_American "Central American") background (7.2% [Nicaraguan](/wiki/Nicaraguan_people "Nicaraguan people"), 5.8% [Honduran](/wiki/Honduran_people "Honduran people"), 1.2% [Salvadoran](/wiki/Salvadoran_people "Salvadoran people"), and 1.0% [Guatemalan](/wiki/Guatemalan_people "Guatemalan people")), 8.7% were of [South American](/wiki/South_America "South America") descent (3.2% Colombian, 1.4% [Venezuelan](/wiki/Venezuelan_people "Venezuelan people"), 1.2% [Peruvian](/wiki/Peruvian_people "Peruvian people"), 1.2% [Argentine](/wiki/Argentine_people "Argentine people"), 1.0% [Chilean](/wiki/Chilean_people "Chilean people") and 0.7% [Ecuadorian](/wiki/Ecuadorian_people "Ecuadorian people")), 4.0% had [other Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_people "Hispanic people") origins (0.5% [Spaniard](/wiki/Spaniard "Spaniard")), 3.2% descended from [Puerto Ricans](/wiki/Puerto_Rican_people "Puerto Rican people"), 2.4% were [Dominican](/wiki/People_of_the_Dominican_Republic "People of the Dominican Republic"), and 1.5% had Mexican ancestry.

In 2010, 5.6% of city residents were [West Indian](/wiki/West_Indian "West Indian") or [Afro-Caribbean American](/wiki/Afro-Caribbean_American "Afro-Caribbean American") origin (4.4% [Haitian](/wiki/Haitian_people "Haitian people"), 0.4% [Jamaican](/wiki/Jamaican_people "Jamaican people"), 0.4% [Bahamian](/wiki/Bahamian_people "Bahamian people"), 0.1% [British West Indian](/wiki/British_West_Indian "British West Indian"), and 0.1% [Trinidadian and Tobagonian](/wiki/Trinidadian_and_Tobagonian "Trinidadian and Tobagonian"), 0.1% [Other or Unspecified West Indian](/wiki/Afro-Caribbean "Afro-Caribbean")),[[117]](#cite_note-128) 3.0% were [Black Hispanics](/wiki/Black_Hispanic "Black Hispanic"),[[118]](#cite_note-HiLaMi-129) and 0.4% were [Subsaharan African](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_African "Sub-Saharan African") origin.[[119]](#cite_note-MIAMIdemo-130)[[120]](#cite_note-MIApop-131)

In 2010, those of (non-Hispanic white) European ancestry were 11.9% of Miami's population. Of the city's total population, 1.7% were German, 1.6% Italian, 1.4% Irish, 1.0% English, 0.8% French, 0.6% Russian, and 0.5% were Polish.[[119]](#cite_note-MIAMIdemo-130)[[120]](#cite_note-MIApop-131)

In 2010, those of Asian ancestry were 1.0% of Miami's population. Of the city's total population, 0.3% were [Indian](/wiki/Indian_Americans "Indian Americans")/[Indo-Caribbean](/wiki/Indo-Caribbean_American "Indo-Caribbean American") (1,206 people), 0.3% Chinese/[Chinese Caribbean](/wiki/Chinese_Caribbeans "Chinese Caribbeans") (1,804 people), 0.2% [Filipino](/wiki/Filipino_people "Filipino people") (647 people), 0.1% were other Asian (433 people), 0.1% Japanese (245 people), 0.1% Korean (213 people), and 0.0% were [Vietnamese](/wiki/Vietnamese_people "Vietnamese people") (125 people).[[119]](#cite_note-MIAMIdemo-130)

In 2010, 1.9% of the population considered themselves to be of only American ancestry (regardless of race or ethnicity),[[119]](#cite_note-MIAMIdemo-130)[[120]](#cite_note-MIApop-131) while 0.5% were of Arab ancestry, in 2010.[[119]](#cite_note-MIAMIdemo-130)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Cathedral_of_Saint_Mary_-_Miami_08.jpg/250px-Cathedral_of_Saint_Mary_-_Miami_08.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cathedral_of_Saint_Mary_-_Miami_08.jpg)

[Cathedral of Saint Mary](/wiki/Cathedral_of_Saint_Mary_(Miami) "Cathedral of Saint Mary (Miami)"), the seat of the [archbishop](/wiki/Archbishop "Archbishop") of the [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Miami](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Miami "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Miami").

In a 2014 [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") study, Christianity was the most-practiced religion in Miami (68%), with 39% professing attendance at a variety of churches that could be considered [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant"), and 27% professing [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church").[[122]](#cite_note-133)[[123]](#cite_note-134) Followed by Judaism (9%); [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), and a variety of other religions have smaller followings; [atheism](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism") or no self-identifying organized religious affiliation was practiced by 21%.

There has been a [Norwegian Seamen's church](/wiki/Norwegian_Church_Abroad "Norwegian Church Abroad") in Miami since the early 1980s. In November 2011, [Crown Princess of Norway Mette-Marit](/wiki/Mette-Marit,_Crown_Princess_of_Norway "Mette-Marit, Crown Princess of Norway") opened a new building for the church. The church was built as a center for the 10,000 Scandinavians that live in Florida. Around 4,000 of them are [Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegians "Norwegians"). The church is also an important place for the 150 Norwegians that work at [Walt Disney World](/wiki/Walt_Disney_World "Walt Disney World") in Central Florida.[[124]](#cite_note-135)

In a 2022 [Point-In-Time](/wiki/Point-In-Time_Count "Point-In-Time Count") Homeless Count, there were 3,440 [homeless](/wiki/Homelessness "Homelessness") people in Miami-Dade County,[[125]](#cite_note-136) 970 of which were on the streets. In the city limits of Miami, there were 591 unsheltered homeless people on the streets, up from 555 in 2021.[[126]](#cite_note-137)

According to [National Immigration Forum](/wiki/National_Immigration_Forum "National Immigration Forum"), the top countries of origin for Miami's immigrants are Latin America (86%): Cuba (741,666), Haiti (213,000), Colombia (166,338), Jamaica (144,445); Europe (6.1%): United Kingdom (23,334), Germany (15,611), Italy (14,240) and Asia (5.2%): India (23,602), China (21,580) and the Philippines (15,078).[[127]](#cite_note-138)

## Economy

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Brickell1.JPG/250px-Brickell1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Brickell1.JPG)

[Brickell Avenue](/wiki/Brickell_Avenue "Brickell Avenue") has the largest concentration of international banks in the nation.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Brickell_neighborhood_skyline_%2860062p%29.jpg/250px-Brickell_neighborhood_skyline_%2860062p%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brickell_neighborhood_skyline_(60062p).jpg)

Downtown Miami's [Brickell Financial District](/wiki/Brickell "Brickell") serves as an example of the city's recent waves of "[Manhattanization](/wiki/Manhattanization "Manhattanization")".

Miami is a major center of commerce and finance and has a strong [international business](/wiki/International_business "International business") community. According to the 2020 ranking of world cities undertaken by the [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network") (GaWC) based on the level of presence of global corporate service organizations, Miami is considered a Beta + level [world city](/wiki/Global_city "Global city"), along with [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"), [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas"), and [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"). However, according to the U.S. census between 2015 and 2019, Miami lacks in terms of owner-occupied housing, computer and internet usage, education of bachelor's degree or higher, median household income, per capita income, while having a higher percentage of persons in poverty.[[128]](#cite_note-:0-139)[[129]](#cite_note-140) In 2013, Miami had a Gross Metropolitan Product of $257 billion, ranking 11th in the United States and 20th worldwide in GMP.[[130]](#cite_note-141)[[131]](#cite_note-142)

Several large companies are headquartered in Miami and the Miami area, including but not limited to [Akerman LLP](/wiki/Akerman_LLP "Akerman LLP"),[[132]](#cite_note-143) [Alienware](/wiki/Alienware "Alienware"),[[133]](#cite_note-144) [Arquitectonica](/wiki/Arquitectonica "Arquitectonica"),[[134]](#cite_note-145) [Brightstar Corporation](/wiki/Brightstar_Corporation "Brightstar Corporation"), [Celebrity Cruises](/wiki/Celebrity_Cruises "Celebrity Cruises"),[[135]](#cite_note-146) [Carnival Corporation](/wiki/Carnival_Corporation_%26_plc "Carnival Corporation & plc"),[[136]](#cite_note-147) [Duany Plater-Zyberk](/wiki/Duany_Plater-Zyberk "Duany Plater-Zyberk"),[[137]](#cite_note-148) [Greenberg Traurig](/wiki/Greenberg_Traurig "Greenberg Traurig"), Inktel Direct, [Lennar Corporation](/wiki/Lennar_Corporation "Lennar Corporation"), [Norwegian Cruise Line](/wiki/Norwegian_Cruise_Line "Norwegian Cruise Line"), [Oceania Cruises](/wiki/Oceania_Cruises "Oceania Cruises"), [OPKO Health](/wiki/OPKO_Health "OPKO Health"), [Parkjockey](/wiki/Parkjockey "Parkjockey"), [RCTV International](/wiki/RCTV_International "RCTV International"),[[138]](#cite_note-149) [Royal Caribbean International](/wiki/Royal_Caribbean_International "Royal Caribbean International"), [Sitel](/wiki/Sitel "Sitel"), [Southern Wine & Spirits](/wiki/Southern_Wine_%26_Spirits "Southern Wine & Spirits"),[[139]](#cite_note-150) [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo"), [Vector Group](/wiki/Vector_Group "Vector Group"), [Watsco](/wiki/Watsco "Watsco") and [World Fuel Services](/wiki/World_Fuel_Services "World Fuel Services"). In 2022, hedge fund [Citadel LLC](/wiki/Citadel_LLC "Citadel LLC") and [market maker](/wiki/Market_maker "Market maker") [Citadel Securities](/wiki/Citadel_Securities "Citadel Securities") relocated their headquarters from Chicago to Miami.[[140]](#cite_note-ChicagoBusinessExodus1-151)[[141]](#cite_note-ChicagoBusinessExodus2-152)[[142]](#cite_note-ChicagoBusinessExodus3-153) Over 1,400 multinational firms are located in Miami, with many major global organizations headquartering their Latin American operations (or regional offices) in the city including [Walmart](/wiki/Walmart "Walmart").[[143]](#cite_note-154) Companies based in nearby cities or unincorporated areas of [Miami-Dade County](/wiki/Miami-Dade_County,_Florida "Miami-Dade County, Florida") include, [Benihana](/wiki/Benihana "Benihana"), [Burger King](/wiki/Burger_King "Burger King"), [Carnival Cruise Line](/wiki/Carnival_Cruise_Line "Carnival Cruise Line"), [Navarro Discount Pharmacies](/wiki/Navarro_Discount_Pharmacies "Navarro Discount Pharmacies"), [Perry Ellis International](/wiki/Perry_Ellis_International "Perry Ellis International"), [Ryder](/wiki/Ryder "Ryder"), [Sedano's](/wiki/Sedano%27s "Sedano's"), [UniMás](/wiki/UniM%C3%A1s "UniMás"), and [U.S. Century Bank](/wiki/U.S._Century_Bank "U.S. Century Bank").[[144]](#cite_note-155)[[145]](#cite_note-156)[[146]](#cite_note-157)[[147]](#cite_note-158)[[148]](#cite_note-159)[[149]](#cite_note-160)[[150]](#cite_note-161)

Miami is a major television production center, and the most important city in the United States for Spanish-language media. [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo") and [UniMás](/wiki/UniM%C3%A1s "UniMás") have their headquarters in the Miami area. [Univisión Studios](/wiki/Univision "Univision") and [Telemundo Global Studios](/wiki/Telemundo_Global_Studios "Telemundo Global Studios") produce much of the original programming for their respective parent networks, such as [telenovelas](/wiki/Telenovela "Telenovela"), news, sports, and talk shows. In 2011, 85% of Telemundo's original programming was filmed in Miami.[[151]](#cite_note-162) Miami is a significant music recording center, with the [Sony Music Latin](/wiki/Sony_Music_Latin "Sony Music Latin") headquarters in the city,[[152]](#cite_note-163) along with many other smaller record labels. Miami also attracts many artists for music video and film shoots.

During the mid-2000s, Miami witnessed its largest real estate boom since the [Florida land boom of the 1920s](/wiki/Florida_land_boom_of_the_1920s "Florida land boom of the 1920s"), and the city had well over a hundred approved high-rise construction projects. However, only 50 were actually built.[[153]](#cite_note-164) A rapid wave of [Manhattanization](/wiki/Manhattanization "Manhattanization"), or high-rise construction, led to fast population growth in Miami's inner neighborhoods, with [Downtown](/wiki/Greater_Downtown_Miami "Greater Downtown Miami"), [Brickell](/wiki/Brickell "Brickell") and [Edgewater](/wiki/Edgewater_(Miami) "Edgewater (Miami)") becoming the fastest-growing areas of Miami. Miami currently has the seven tallest, as well as fifteen of top twenty, [skyscrapers in the state of Florida](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Florida "List of tallest buildings in Florida"), with the tallest being the 868-foot (265 m) [Panorama Tower](/wiki/Panorama_Tower "Panorama Tower").[[154]](#cite_note-emporis-165)

The [housing market crash of 2007](/wiki/United_States_housing_bubble "United States housing bubble") caused a foreclosure crisis in the area.[[155]](#cite_note-166) Like other metro areas in the United States, crime in Miami is localized to specific neighborhoods.[[156]](#cite_note-167)

[Miami International Airport](/wiki/Miami_International_Airport "Miami International Airport") ([IATA](/wiki/IATA_airport_code "IATA airport code"): MIA) and [PortMiami](/wiki/PortMiami "PortMiami") are among the nation's busiest ports of entry, especially for cargo from [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America") and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"). PortMiami is the world's busiest cruise port. Miami International Airport is the busiest airport in Florida and the largest gateway between the United States and Latin America.[[157]](#cite_note-168) Due to its strength in international business, finance and trade, Miami has among the largest concentration of international banks in the country, primarily along [Brickell Avenue](/wiki/Brickell_Avenue "Brickell Avenue") in [Brickell](/wiki/Brickell "Brickell"), Miami's financial district. Miami was the host city of the 2003 [Free Trade Area of the Americas](/wiki/Free_Trade_Area_of_the_Americas "Free Trade Area of the Americas") negotiations.

Miami is the home to the [National Hurricane Center](/wiki/National_Hurricane_Center "National Hurricane Center") and the headquarters of the [United States Southern Command](/wiki/United_States_Southern_Command "United States Southern Command"), responsible for military operations in [Central](/wiki/Central_America "Central America") and South America. Miami is also an industrial center, especially for stone quarrying and warehousing. These industries are centered largely on the western fringes of Miami near [Doral](/wiki/Doral,_Florida "Doral, Florida") and [Hialeah](/wiki/Hialeah,_Florida "Hialeah, Florida").[[158]](#cite_note-169)

In the 2012 census, Miami had the [fourth highest percentage](/wiki/List_of_lowest-income_places_in_the_United_States "List of lowest-income places in the United States") of family incomes below the federal poverty line out of all large cities in the United States, behind [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"), [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"), and [Cincinnati](/wiki/Cincinnati "Cincinnati"), respectively. Miami is also one of the very few cities in the U.S. where the local government has gone bankrupt, in 2001.[[159]](#cite_note-170)

The Little Fire Ant, *[Wasmannia auropunctata](/wiki/Wasmannia_auropunctata "Wasmannia auropunctata")*, is an [invasive agricultural pest](/wiki/Invasive_agricultural_pest "Invasive agricultural pest") in parts of Miami.[[160]](#cite_note-LFA-171)

### PortMiami

Main article: [PortMiami](/wiki/PortMiami "PortMiami")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/PortMiami_main_channel_with_city_skyline_in_the_background_%28March_11%2C_2024%29.jpg/250px-PortMiami_main_channel_with_city_skyline_in_the_background_%28March_11%2C_2024%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:PortMiami_main_channel_with_city_skyline_in_the_background_(March_11,_2024).jpg)

[PortMiami](/wiki/PortMiami "PortMiami"), the world's largest cruise ship port and headquarters for many of the world's largest cruise companies, seen from the docked *[MSC Magnifica](/wiki/MSC_Magnifica "MSC Magnifica")* cruise ship in March 2024.

[PortMiami](/wiki/PortMiami "PortMiami") in Miami is the largest cruise ship port in the world.[[161]](#cite_note-miamiport-172) It has retained its status as the number one cruise and passenger port in the world for well over a decade, accommodating the largest cruise ships and the major cruise lines. In 2017, the port served 5,340,559 cruise passengers.[[162]](#cite_note-portreport-173) The port is one of the nation's busiest cargo ports, importing 9,162,340 tons of cargo in 2017.[[162]](#cite_note-portreport-173)

Among [North American](/wiki/North_American "North American") ports, it ranks second to [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans")' [Port of South Louisiana](/wiki/Port_of_South_Louisiana "Port of South Louisiana") in cargo tonnage imported from [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"). The port sits on 518 acres (2 km2) and has seven passenger terminals. China is the port's number one import country and number one export country. Miami has the world's largest amount of cruise line headquarters, home to [Carnival Cruise Line](/wiki/Carnival_Cruise_Line "Carnival Cruise Line"), [Celebrity Cruises](/wiki/Celebrity_Cruises "Celebrity Cruises"), [Norwegian Cruise Line](/wiki/Norwegian_Cruise_Line "Norwegian Cruise Line"), [Oceania Cruises](/wiki/Oceania_Cruises "Oceania Cruises"), and [Royal Caribbean International](/wiki/Royal_Caribbean_International "Royal Caribbean International"). In 2014, the [Port of Miami Tunnel](/wiki/Port_of_Miami_Tunnel "Port of Miami Tunnel") opened, connecting the [MacArthur Causeway](/wiki/MacArthur_Causeway "MacArthur Causeway") to PortMiami.[[163]](#cite_note-174)

### Tourism and conventions

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Miami-florida-royal-caribbean-building.jpg/250px-Miami-florida-royal-caribbean-building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miami-florida-royal-caribbean-building.jpg)

The [Royal Caribbean International](/wiki/Royal_Caribbean_International "Royal Caribbean International") headquarters at [PortMiami](/wiki/PortMiami "PortMiami")

Tourism is one of the Miami's largest private-sector industries, accounting for more than 144,800 jobs in [Miami-Dade County](/wiki/Miami-Dade_County,_Florida "Miami-Dade County, Florida").[[164]](#cite_note-175) Miami's frequent portrayal in music, film, and popular culture has made Miami and its landmarks recognizable worldwide. In 2016, it attracted the second-highest number of foreign tourists of any city in the United States, after New York City, and is among the top 20 cities worldwide by international visitor spending. More than 15.9 million visitors arrived in Miami in 2017, adding $26.1 billion to the economy.[[165]](#cite_note-176) With a large hotel infrastructure and the newly renovated [Miami Beach Convention Center](/wiki/Miami_Beach_Convention_Center "Miami Beach Convention Center"), Miami is a popular destination for annual conventions and conferences.[[166]](#cite_note-177)

Some of the most popular tourist destinations in Miami include [South Beach](/wiki/South_Beach "South Beach"), [Lincoln Road](/wiki/Lincoln_Road "Lincoln Road"), [Bayside Marketplace](/wiki/Bayside_Marketplace "Bayside Marketplace"), [Downtown Miami](/wiki/Greater_Downtown_Miami "Greater Downtown Miami"), and [Brickell City Centre](/wiki/Brickell_City_Centre "Brickell City Centre"). The [Art Deco District](/wiki/Miami_Beach_Architectural_District "Miami Beach Architectural District") in [Miami Beach](/wiki/Miami_Beach,_Florida "Miami Beach, Florida") is reputed as one of the most glamorous in the world for its [nightclubs](/wiki/Nightclubs "Nightclubs"), beaches, historical buildings, and shopping. Annual events such as the [Miami Open](/wiki/Miami_Open_(tennis) "Miami Open (tennis)"), [Art Basel](/wiki/Art_Basel "Art Basel"), the [Winter Music Conference](/wiki/Winter_Music_Conference "Winter Music Conference"), the [South Beach Wine and Food Festival](/wiki/Food_Network_South_Beach_Wine_and_Food_Festival "Food Network South Beach Wine and Food Festival"), and [Mercedes-Benz Fashion Week Miami](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_Fashion_Week_Miami "Mercedes-Benz Fashion Week Miami") attract millions to the metropolis every year.

## Culture

See also: [LGBT culture in Miami](/wiki/LGBT_culture_in_Miami "LGBT culture in Miami"), [List of films and television shows set in Miami](/wiki/List_of_films_and_television_shows_set_in_Miami "List of films and television shows set in Miami"), [Music of Miami](/wiki/Music_of_Miami "Music of Miami"), and [Nicknames of Miami](/wiki/Nicknames_of_Miami "Nicknames of Miami")

Miami enjoys a vibrant culture that is influenced by a diverse population from all around the world. Miami is known as the "Magic City" for seemingly popping up overnight due to its young age and massive growth. Miami is infamous for its [drug war in the early 1980s](/wiki/Miami_drug_war "Miami drug war") and its outrun aesthetics.[[167]](#cite_note-178)[[168]](#cite_note-179)[[169]](#cite_note-180) It is [nicknamed](/wiki/Nicknames_of_Miami "Nicknames of Miami") the "Capital of Latin America" because of its high population of Spanish speakers.[[170]](#cite_note-181)[[171]](#cite_note-182)[[172]](#cite_note-183)[[173]](#cite_note-184)

Miami has been the setting of numerous films and television shows, including *[Bad Boys](/wiki/Bad_Boys_(franchise) "Bad Boys (franchise)")*, *[Miami Vice](/wiki/Miami_Vice "Miami Vice")*, *[Cocaine ### Entertainment

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Knightconcerthall.jpg/250px-Knightconcerthall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Knightconcerthall.jpg)

The [Adrienne Arsht Center for the Performing Arts](/wiki/Adrienne_Arsht_Center_for_the_Performing_Arts "Adrienne Arsht Center for the Performing Arts"), the second-largest performing arts center in the United States

### Venues

Miami is home to many entertainment venues, theaters, museums, parks, and performing arts centers. The [Adrienne Arsht Center for the Performing Arts](/wiki/Adrienne_Arsht_Center_for_the_Performing_Arts "Adrienne Arsht Center for the Performing Arts") is the home of the [Florida Grand Opera](/wiki/Florida_Grand_Opera "Florida Grand Opera") and the second-largest performing arts center in the United States after [Lincoln Center](/wiki/Lincoln_Center_for_the_Performing_Arts "Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts") in New York City.[[183]](#cite_note-194) The center attracts many large-scale operas, ballets, concerts, and musicals from around the world.

Other performing arts venues in Miami include the [Olympia Theater](/wiki/Olympia_Theater_(Miami) "Olympia Theater (Miami)"), [Wertheim Performing Arts Center](/wiki/Wertheim_Performing_Arts_Center "Wertheim Performing Arts Center"), the [Fair Expo Center](/wiki/Fair_Expo_Center "Fair Expo Center"), the [Tower Theater](/wiki/Tower_Theater_(Miami,_Florida) "Tower Theater (Miami, Florida)"), and the [Bayfront Park Amphitheater](/wiki/Bayfront_Park "Bayfront Park").[[184]](#cite_note-195)

Miami's first boat-in [movie theater](/wiki/Movie_theater "Movie theater") opened in 2020.[[185]](#cite_note-miamiherald.com-196)

### Events

Miami is home to a number of annual festivals, such as the [Calle Ocho Festival](/wiki/Calle_Ocho_Festival "Calle Ocho Festival"), the largest Latin music festival in the country, which has run since 1978.[[186]](#cite_note-197)[[187]](#cite_note-198)

Another celebrated event is the [Miami International Film Festival](/wiki/Miami_International_Film_Festival "Miami International Film Festival"), taking place every year for 10 days around the first week of March, during which independent international and American films are screened across Miami. Miami has over a half dozen independent film theaters.[[188]](#cite_note-199)

The Miami Jewish Film Festival (MJFF) was established in 1996.[[189]](#cite_note-200) It is an annual event held in January that screens a variety of films relating to Jewish history and culture. As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Miami&action=edit), the festival awards a number of film awards: Critics Jury Prize, Next Wave Jury Prize, Kadima Jury Prize, Torchbearer Award, Emerging Filmmaker Award, and the Audience Awards for Best Narrative Film, Documentary Film, and Short Film. The Miami Jewish Film Club holds special screenings throughout the year.[[190]](#cite_note-201)

Miami is a major fashion center, home to models and some of the top modeling agencies in the world. Miami hosts many fashion shows and events, including the annual [Miami Fashion Week](/wiki/Miami_Fashion_Week "Miami Fashion Week") and the [Mercedes-Benz Fashion Week Miami](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_Fashion_Week_Miami "Mercedes-Benz Fashion Week Miami"), held in the [Wynwood Art District](/wiki/Wynwood_Art_District "Wynwood Art District").[[191]](#cite_note-202)[[192]](#cite_note-203)[[193]](#cite_note-204)

### Music and performing arts

See also: [Music of Miami](/wiki/Music_of_Miami "Music of Miami")

Miami attracts a large number of musicians, singers, actors, dancers, and orchestral players. The city has numerous orchestras, symphonies, and performing art conservatories. These include the [Florida Grand Opera](/wiki/Florida_Grand_Opera "Florida Grand Opera"), [Alhambra Orchestra](/wiki/Alhambra_Orchestra "Alhambra Orchestra"), [Florida International University](/wiki/Florida_International_University "Florida International University")'s Herbert and Nicole Wertheim School of Music and Performing Arts, [Frost School of Music](/wiki/Frost_School_of_Music "Frost School of Music"), and the [New World School of the Arts](/wiki/New_World_School_of_the_Arts "New World School of the Arts").[[194]](#cite_note-205)[[195]](#cite_note-206)[[196]](#cite_note-207)[[197]](#cite_note-208)

### Museums and visual arts

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Lowe.jpg/250px-Lowe.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lowe.jpg)

The [Lowe Art Museum](/wiki/Lowe_Art_Museum "Lowe Art Museum") on the campus of the [University of Miami](/wiki/University_of_Miami "University of Miami")

Some of the museums in Miami include the [Frost Art Museum](/wiki/Frost_Art_Museum "Frost Art Museum"), [Frost Museum of Science](/wiki/Phillip_and_Patricia_Frost_Museum_of_Science "Phillip and Patricia Frost Museum of Science"), [HistoryMiami](/wiki/HistoryMiami "HistoryMiami"), [Institute of Contemporary Art](/wiki/Institute_of_Contemporary_Art_(Miami) "Institute of Contemporary Art (Miami)"), [Miami Children's Museum](/wiki/Miami_Children%27s_Museum "Miami Children's Museum"), [Pérez Art Museum](/wiki/P%C3%A9rez_Art_Museum_Miami "Pérez Art Museum Miami"), [Lowe Art Museum](/wiki/Lowe_Art_Museum "Lowe Art Museum"), and the [Vizcaya Museum and Gardens](/wiki/Vizcaya_Museum_and_Gardens "Vizcaya Museum and Gardens"), a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") set on a 28-acre early 20th century estate in [Coconut Grove](/wiki/Coconut_Grove,_Florida "Coconut Grove, Florida").[[198]](#cite_note-209)

### Cuisine

The cuisine of Miami is a reflection of its diverse population, with a heavy influence from [Latin American](/wiki/Latin_American_cuisine "Latin American cuisine"), [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean_cuisine "Caribbean cuisine"), [Soul](/wiki/Soul_food "Soul food"), and [Jewish cuisine](/wiki/Jewish_cuisine "Jewish cuisine"). By combining them with mainstream [American cuisine](/wiki/American_cuisine "American cuisine"), it has spawned a unique [South Florida](/wiki/South_Florida "South Florida") style of cooking known as [Floribbean cuisine](/wiki/Floribbean_cuisine "Floribbean cuisine"). It is widely available throughout Miami and South Florida and can be found in restaurant chains such as [Pollo Tropical](/wiki/Pollo_Tropical "Pollo Tropical").

Cuban immigrants in the 1960s originated the [Cuban sandwich](/wiki/Cuban_sandwich "Cuban sandwich") and brought [medianoche](/wiki/Medianoche "Medianoche"), [Cuban espresso](/wiki/Cuban_espresso "Cuban espresso"), [Bistec de palomilla](/wiki/Bistec_de_palomilla "Bistec de palomilla"), and [croquetas](/wiki/Croquette "Croquette"), all of which have grown in popularity among all Miamians and have become symbols of the city's varied cuisine. Today, these are part of the local culture and can be found throughout the city at window cafés, particularly outside of supermarkets and restaurants.[[199]](#cite_note-210)[[200]](#cite_note-211) Some of these locations, such as the [Versailles restaurant](/wiki/Versailles_restaurant "Versailles restaurant") in [Little Havana](/wiki/Little_Havana "Little Havana"), are landmark eateries of Miami. Located on the Atlantic Ocean, and with a long history as a [seaport](/wiki/Port "Port"), Miami is also known for its seafood, with many seafood restaurants located along the [Miami River](/wiki/Miami_River_(Florida) "Miami River (Florida)") and in and around Biscayne Bay.[[201]](#cite_note-212) The city is also the headquarters of restaurant chains such as [Burger King](/wiki/Burger_King "Burger King") and [Benihana](/wiki/Benihana "Benihana").

### Dialect

Main article: [Miami accent](/wiki/Miami_accent "Miami accent")

The Miami area has a unique dialect of [American English](/wiki/American_English "American English"), commonly called the "[Miami accent](/wiki/Miami_accent "Miami accent")", that is widely spoken. The accent developed among second- or third-generation [Hispanics](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans"), including [Cuban Americans](/wiki/Cuban_Americans "Cuban Americans"), whose first language was English. Some [non-Hispanic white](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_whites "Non-Hispanic whites"), [black](/wiki/Black_people "Black people") and [other races](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States "Race and ethnicity in the United States") who were born and raised in the Miami area have tended to adopt it as well.[[202]](#cite_note-213)

It is based on a fairly [standard American accent](/wiki/General_American "General American") but with some changes, very similar to dialects in the [Mid-Atlantic](/wiki/Mid-Atlantic_(United_States) "Mid-Atlantic (United States)"), especially those in the [New York area](/wiki/New_York_City_English "New York City English") and [Northern New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey_English "New Jersey English"), including [New York Latino English](/wiki/New_York_Latino_English "New York Latino English"). Unlike [Virginia Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont_region_of_Virginia "Piedmont region of Virginia"), Coastal Southern American, Northeast American dialects and [Florida Cracker](/wiki/Florida_Cracker "Florida Cracker") dialect, "Miami accent" is [rhotic](/wiki/Rhoticity_in_English "Rhoticity in English"). It incorporates a rhythm and pronunciation heavily influenced by Spanish, where rhythm is [syllable-timed](/wiki/Isochrony#syllable_timing "Isochrony").[[203]](#cite_note-214)

This is a native dialect of English, not learner English or [interlanguage](/wiki/Interlanguage "Interlanguage"). It is possible to differentiate this variety from an [interlanguage](/wiki/Interlanguage "Interlanguage") spoken by second-language speakers, in that the "Miami accent" does *not* generally display the following features: there is no [addition](/wiki/Epenthesis "Epenthesis") of /ɛ/ before initial consonant clusters with /s/, speakers do not confuse /dʒ/ with /j/, (e.g., *Yale* with *jail*), and /r/ and /rr/ are pronounced as [alveolar approximant [ɹ]](/wiki/Alveolar_approximant "Alveolar approximant") instead of [alveolar tap](/wiki/Alveolar_tap "Alveolar tap") [ɾ] or [alveolar trill](/wiki/Alveolar_trill "Alveolar trill") [r] in Spanish.[[204]](#cite_note-215)[[205]](#cite_note-216)[[206]](#cite_note-217)[[207]](#cite_note-218)

## Sports

Main article: [Sports in Miami](/wiki/Sports_in_Miami "Sports in Miami")

See also: [Florida Panthers](/wiki/Florida_Panthers "Florida Panthers"), [Inter Miami CF](/wiki/Inter_Miami_CF "Inter Miami CF"), [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins"), [Miami Heat](/wiki/Miami_Heat "Miami Heat"), [Miami Hurricanes](/wiki/Miami_Hurricanes "Miami Hurricanes"), and [Miami Marlins](/wiki/Miami_Marlins "Miami Marlins")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/200127-H-PX819-0092.jpg/250px-200127-H-PX819-0092.jpg)](/wiki/File:200127-H-PX819-0092.jpg)

[Hard Rock Stadium](/wiki/Hard_Rock_Stadium "Hard Rock Stadium") is the home field for the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League"), the [Miami Hurricanes football team](/wiki/Miami_Hurricanes_football "Miami Hurricanes football") of the [University of Miami](/wiki/University_of_Miami "University of Miami"), and [College Football Playoff](/wiki/College_Football_Playoff "College Football Playoff")'s [Orange Bowl](/wiki/Orange_Bowl "Orange Bowl") game held annually each January.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Marlins_First_Pitch_at_Marlins_Park%2C_April_4%2C_2012_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Marlins_First_Pitch_at_Marlins_Park%2C_April_4%2C_2012_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marlins_First_Pitch_at_Marlins_Park,_April_4,_2012_(cropped).jpg)

[LoanDepot Park](/wiki/LoanDepot_Park "LoanDepot Park"), home of the [Miami Marlins](/wiki/Miami_Marlins "Miami Marlins") of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball")

Miami's main five sports teams are [Inter Miami](/wiki/Inter_Miami "Inter Miami") of [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") (MLS),[[208]](#cite_note-219) the [Miami Marlins](/wiki/Miami_Marlins "Miami Marlins") of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") (MLB),[[209]](#cite_note-220) the [Florida Panthers](/wiki/Florida_Panthers "Florida Panthers") of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL),[[210]](#cite_note-221) the [Miami Heat](/wiki/Miami_Heat "Miami Heat") of the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA)[[211]](#cite_note-222) and the [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL).[[212]](#cite_note-223) The [Miami Open](/wiki/Miami_Open_(tennis) "Miami Open (tennis)"), an annual tennis tournament, was previously held in [Key Biscayne](/wiki/Key_Biscayne,_Florida "Key Biscayne, Florida") before moving to [Hard Rock Stadium](/wiki/Hard_Rock_Stadium "Hard Rock Stadium") after the tournament was purchased by [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins") owner [Stephen Ross](/wiki/Stephen_M._Ross "Stephen M. Ross") in 2019.

Miami is home to numerous [marinas](/wiki/Marina "Marina"), [jai alai](/wiki/Jai_alai "Jai alai") venues, and [golf courses](/wiki/Golf_course "Golf course"). The city streets have hosted professional auto races in the past, most notably the open-wheel [Grand Prix of Miami](/wiki/Grand_Prix_of_Miami_(open_wheel_racing) "Grand Prix of Miami (open wheel racing)"), the sports car [Grand Prix of Miami](/wiki/Grand_Prix_of_Miami_(sports_car_racing) "Grand Prix of Miami (sports car racing)"), and [Miami Grand Prix](/wiki/Miami_Grand_Prix "Miami Grand Prix") of [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One").[[213]](#cite_note-224) In 2015, Miami hosted [a one-off Formula E race](/wiki/2015_Miami_ePrix "2015 Miami ePrix"). The [Homestead–Miami Speedway](/wiki/Homestead%E2%80%93Miami_Speedway "Homestead–Miami Speedway") oval [hosts](/wiki/NASCAR_Cup_Series_at_Homestead%E2%80%93Miami "NASCAR Cup Series at Homestead–Miami") [NASCAR](/wiki/NASCAR "NASCAR") races.[[214]](#cite_note-225)

The Heat, Marlins, and Inter Miami play within Miami's city limits, at the [Kaseya Center](/wiki/Kaseya_Center "Kaseya Center") in [Downtown](/wiki/Greater_Downtown_Miami "Greater Downtown Miami"), [LoanDepot Park](/wiki/LoanDepot_Park "LoanDepot Park") in [Little Havana](/wiki/Little_Havana "Little Havana"), and [Nu Stadium](/wiki/Nu_Stadium "Nu Stadium") in [Grapeland Heights](/wiki/Grapeland_Heights "Grapeland Heights") respectively. Marlins Park is built on the site of the old [Miami Orange Bowl](/wiki/Miami_Orange_Bowl "Miami Orange Bowl") stadium.[[215]](#cite_note-226)

The [Miami Dolphins](/wiki/Miami_Dolphins "Miami Dolphins") play at [Hard Rock Stadium](/wiki/Hard_Rock_Stadium "Hard Rock Stadium") in suburban [Miami Gardens](/wiki/Miami_Gardens,_Florida "Miami Gardens, Florida"), while the [Florida Panthers](/wiki/Florida_Panthers "Florida Panthers") play in nearby [Sunrise](/wiki/Sunrise,_Florida "Sunrise, Florida") at [Amerant Bank Arena](/wiki/Amerant_Bank_Arena "Amerant Bank Arena"). [Miami FC](/wiki/Miami_FC "Miami FC") is another professional soccer club that plays in the [USL Championship](/wiki/USL_Championship "USL Championship") second tier of the [United States soccer league system](/wiki/United_States_soccer_league_system "United States soccer league system"). The Club plays its home matches at the [Pitbull Stadium](/wiki/Pitbull_Stadium "Pitbull Stadium") on the campus of [Florida International University](/wiki/Florida_International_University "Florida International University") (FIU) in Miami.

The [Orange Bowl](/wiki/Orange_Bowl "Orange Bowl"), one of the major bowl games in the [College Football Playoff](/wiki/College_Football_Playoff "College Football Playoff") of the [NCAA](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association"), is played at Hard Rock Stadium every winter. The stadium has also hosted the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"). The Miami metro area has hosted the game ten times, five times at the current Hard Rock Stadium and five at the [Miami Orange Bowl](/wiki/Miami_Orange_Bowl "Miami Orange Bowl"), tying New Orleans for the most games.[[216]](#cite_note-227)

Miami is also the home of many college sports teams. The two largest are the [University of Miami](/wiki/University_of_Miami "University of Miami") [Hurricanes](/wiki/Miami_Hurricanes "Miami Hurricanes"), whose [football team](/wiki/Miami_Hurricanes_football "Miami Hurricanes football") plays at [Hard Rock Stadium](/wiki/Hard_Rock_Stadium "Hard Rock Stadium") and [Florida International University](/wiki/Florida_International_University "Florida International University") [Panthers](/wiki/FIU_Panthers "FIU Panthers"), whose [football team](/wiki/FIU_Panthers_football "FIU Panthers football") plays at Pitbull Stadium. The Hurricanes compete in the [Atlantic Coast Conference](/wiki/Atlantic_Coast_Conference "Atlantic Coast Conference") (ACC), while the Panthers compete in the [Conference USA](/wiki/Conference_USA "Conference USA") of the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association").[[217]](#cite_note-228)[[218]](#cite_note-229)

Miami is home to [Paso Fino](/wiki/Paso_Fino "Paso Fino") horses. Competitions are held at Tropical Park Equestrian Center.[[219]](#cite_note-230)

Miami hosted the [2024 Copa América](/wiki/2024_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2024 Copa América") final in July 2024.[[220]](#cite_note-231)

Miami will serve as one of eleven U.S. host cities for the [2026 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup "2026 FIFA World Cup").[[221]](#cite_note-232)
