## Beaches and parks

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Bayfront_Park_-_panoramio_%285%29.jpg/250px-Bayfront_Park_-_panoramio_%285%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bayfront_Park_-_panoramio_(5).jpg)

[Bayfront Park](/wiki/Bayfront_Park "Bayfront Park") on [Biscayne Bay](/wiki/Biscayne_Bay "Biscayne Bay"), February 2017

The City of Miami has various lands operated by the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service"), the Florida Division of Recreation and Parks, and the City of Miami Department of Parks and Recreation.

Miami's tropical weather allows for year-round outdoor activities.[[232]](#cite_note-243) Miami has numerous marinas, rivers, bays, canals, and the Atlantic Ocean, which make boating, canoeing, sailing, and fishing popular outdoor activities. Biscayne Bay has numerous [coral reefs](/wiki/Coral_reef "Coral reef") that make [snorkeling](/wiki/Snorkeling "Snorkeling") and [scuba diving](/wiki/Scuba_diving "Scuba diving") popular. There are over 80 parks and gardens in the city.[[233]](#cite_note-244) The largest and most popular parks are [Bayfront Park](/wiki/Bayfront_Park "Bayfront Park") and [Museum Park](/wiki/Museum_Park_(Miami) "Museum Park (Miami)") (located in the heart of [Downtown](/wiki/Greater_Downtown_Miami "Greater Downtown Miami") and the location of the [Miami-Dade Arena](/wiki/Miami-Dade_Arena "Miami-Dade Arena") and [Bayside Marketplace](/wiki/Bayside_Marketplace "Bayside Marketplace")), [Tropical Park](/wiki/Tropical_Park "Tropical Park"), [Peacock Park](/wiki/Peacock_Park "Peacock Park"), [Virginia Key](/wiki/Virginia_Key "Virginia Key"), and [Watson Island](/wiki/Watson_Island "Watson Island").[[234]](#cite_note-245)[[235]](#cite_note-246)[[236]](#cite_note-247)[[237]](#cite_note-248)[[238]](#cite_note-249)

Other popular cultural destinations in or near Miami include [Zoo Miami](/wiki/Zoo_Miami "Zoo Miami"),[[239]](#cite_note-250) [Jungle Island](/wiki/Jungle_Island "Jungle Island"),[[240]](#cite_note-251) the [Miami Seaquarium](/wiki/Miami_Seaquarium "Miami Seaquarium"),[[241]](#cite_note-252) [Monkey Jungle](/wiki/Monkey_Jungle "Monkey Jungle"),[[242]](#cite_note-253) [Coral Castle](/wiki/Coral_Castle "Coral Castle"),[[243]](#cite_note-254) [Charles Deering Estate](/wiki/Charles_Deering_Estate "Charles Deering Estate"),[[244]](#cite_note-255) [Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden](/wiki/Fairchild_Tropical_Botanic_Garden "Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden"), and [Key Biscayne](/wiki/Key_Biscayne "Key Biscayne").[[245]](#cite_note-256)[[246]](#cite_note-257)

In its 2020 ParkScore ranking, [The Trust for Public Land](/wiki/The_Trust_for_Public_Land "The Trust for Public Land") reported that the park system in the City of Miami was the 64th best park system among the 100 most populous U.S. cities,[[247]](#cite_note-258) down slightly from 48th place in the 2017 ranking.[[248]](#cite_note-259) The City of Miami was analyzed to have a median park size of 2.6 acres, park land as percent of city area of 6.5%, 87% of residents living within a 10-minute walk of a park, $48.39 spending per capita of park services, and 1.3 playgrounds per 10,000 residents.[[249]](#cite_note-260)

## Law and government

Main article: [Government of the City of Miami](/wiki/Government_of_the_City_of_Miami "Government of the City of Miami")

See also: [List of mayors of Miami](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Miami "List of mayors of Miami")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Miami_FL_Pan_Am_Bldg_city_hall02.jpg/250px-Miami_FL_Pan_Am_Bldg_city_hall02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miami_FL_Pan_Am_Bldg_city_hall02.jpg)

[Miami City Hall](/wiki/Miami_City_Hall "Miami City Hall"), located at [Dinner Key](/wiki/Dinner_Key "Dinner Key") in [Coconut Grove](/wiki/Coconut_Grove "Coconut Grove"), is home to Miami's primary administrative offices.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Logo_of_Miami%2C_Florida.svg/250px-Logo_of_Miami%2C_Florida.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Logo_of_Miami,_Florida.svg)

Miami's new logo

The government of the City of Miami uses the [mayor-commissioner](/wiki/Mayor-council_government "Mayor-council government") type of system. The city commission consists of five commissioners that are elected from single member districts. The city commission constitutes the governing body with powers to pass ordinances, adopt regulations, and exercise all powers conferred upon the city in the city charter. The mayor is elected at large and appoints a city manager. The City of Miami is governed by Mayor [Eileen Higgins](/wiki/Eileen_Higgins "Eileen Higgins") and 5 city commissioners that oversee the five districts in the city.[[250]](#cite_note-261)

The commission's regular meetings are held at [Miami City Hall](/wiki/Miami_City_Hall "Miami City Hall"), which is located at 3500 Pan American Drive on [Dinner Key](/wiki/Dinner_Key "Dinner Key") in the neighborhood of [Coconut Grove](/wiki/Coconut_Grove,_Miami,_Florida "Coconut Grove, Miami, Florida"). In the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), Miami is represented by [Republicans](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") [Maria Elvira Salazar](/wiki/Maria_Elvira_Salazar "Maria Elvira Salazar") and [Mario Diaz-Balart](/wiki/Mario_Diaz-Balart "Mario Diaz-Balart"), along with [Democrat](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") [Frederica Wilson](/wiki/Frederica_Wilson "Frederica Wilson").

## Politics

For much of the 20th century, Miami and its surrounding area, [Miami-Dade County](/wiki/Miami-Dade_County,_Florida "Miami-Dade County, Florida"), were solidly [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"), reflecting the city's diverse population and liberal political leanings. However, in recent election cycles, a notable shift has occurred. The 2020 presidential election marked a turning point, with Trump making substantial gains among Hispanic voters, especially within the Cuban-American community. This shift was reflected in Miami-Dade County, where Trump only lost the county by seven points and lost the city by 19 points, a 21-point shift to the right from 2016.[[251]](#cite_note-262) Then in the [2022 Florida gubernatorial election](/wiki/2022_Florida_gubernatorial_election "2022 Florida gubernatorial election"), [Ron DeSantis](/wiki/Ron_DeSantis "Ron DeSantis") won the county for the first time for a [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") candidate since 2002 and only lost the city of Miami by one-and-a-half points.[[252]](#cite_note-263) That trend continued in the [2024 presidential election in Florida](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election_in_Florida "2024 United States presidential election in Florida"), with Trump winning the county by eleven points and losing the city by less than a point,[[253]](#cite_note-Country-264) a rare occurrence for a Republican presidential candidate. This was the first time a Republican candidate had won Miami-Dade County since [1988](/wiki/1988_United_States_presidential_election_in_Florida "1988 United States presidential election in Florida"), a major political transformation in what had traditionally been a Democratic stronghold.[[254]](#cite_note-265) Trump came within 1,000 votes of flipping the city of Miami.[[255]](#cite_note-266)

## Education

See also: [Miami-Dade Public Library System](/wiki/Miami-Dade_Public_Library_System "Miami-Dade Public Library System")

### Colleges and universities

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/University_of_Miami_Otto_G._Richter_Library.jpg/250px-University_of_Miami_Otto_G._Richter_Library.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Miami_Otto_G._Richter_Library.jpg)

Founded in 1925, the [University of Miami](/wiki/University_of_Miami "University of Miami") in nearby [Coral Gables](/wiki/Coral_Gables,_Florida "Coral Gables, Florida") is Florida's top ranked private institution of higher education.

Miami-Dade County has over 200,000 students enrolled in local colleges and universities, placing it seventh in the nation in per capita university enrollment. In 2010, the city's four largest colleges and universities, [Miami Dade College](/wiki/Miami_Dade_College "Miami Dade College"), [Florida International University](/wiki/Florida_International_University "Florida International University"), [University of Miami](/wiki/University_of_Miami "University of Miami"), and [Barry University](/wiki/Barry_University "Barry University"), graduated 28,000 students.[[257]](#cite_note-268)

Miami is also home to both for-profit and nonprofit organizations that offer a range of professional training and other, related educational programs. [*Per* Scholas](/wiki/Per_Scholas "Per Scholas"), for example is a nonprofit organization that offers free professional certification training directed towards successfully passing [CompTIA](/wiki/CompTIA "CompTIA") A+ and Network+ certification exams as a route to securing jobs and building careers.[[258]](#cite_note-269)
[[259]](#cite_note-270)[[260]](#cite_note-271)

* [University of Fort Lauderdale](/wiki/University_of_Fort_Lauderdale "University of Fort Lauderdale"), [Lauderhill](/wiki/Lauderhill,_Florida "Lauderhill, Florida")[[275]](#cite_note-286)
* [University of Miami](/wiki/University_of_Miami "University of Miami"), [Coral Gables](/wiki/Coral_Gables,_Florida "Coral Gables, Florida")[[276]](#cite_note-287)

### Primary and secondary schools

Main article: [Miami-Dade County Public Schools](/wiki/Miami-Dade_County_Public_Schools "Miami-Dade County Public Schools")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Miami_Senior_High_School_July_2013.jpg/250px-Miami_Senior_High_School_July_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miami_Senior_High_School_July_2013.jpg)

[Miami Senior High School](/wiki/Miami_Senior_High_School "Miami Senior High School"), founded in 1903, was Miami's first high school.

Public schools in Miami are governed by [Miami-Dade County Public Schools](/wiki/Miami-Dade_County_Public_Schools "Miami-Dade County Public Schools"), which is the largest school district in Florida and the fourth-largest in the United States. In September 2008 it had a student enrollment of 385,655 and over 392 schools and centers. The district is the largest minority public school system in the country, with 60% of its students being of Hispanic origin, 28% [Black](/wiki/African_American "African American") or [West Indian American](/wiki/West_Indian_American "West Indian American"), 10% [White (non-Hispanic)](/wiki/White_American "White American") and 2% non-white of other minorities.[[277]](#cite_note-288)

The Miami city limits is home to several key high schools: [Design and Architecture High School](/wiki/Design_and_Architecture_High_School "Design and Architecture High School"), ranked seventh highest on the "Gold Medal" by list *[US News and World Report](/wiki/US_News_and_World_Report "US News and World Report")*,[[278]](#cite_note-USNHSlist2007-289) [MAST Academy](/wiki/MAST_Academy "MAST Academy"), [Coral Reef High School](/wiki/Coral_Reef_High_School "Coral Reef High School"), and the [New World School of the Arts](/wiki/New_World_School_of_the_Arts "New World School of the Arts").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] M-DCPS is also one of a few public school districts in the United States to offer optional [bilingual education](/wiki/Bilingual_education "Bilingual education") in Spanish, French, German, [Haitian Creole](/wiki/Haitian_Creole "Haitian Creole"), and [Mandarin Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese").

Miami is home to several well-known Roman Catholic, Jewish and non-denominational private schools. The [Archdiocese of Miami](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Miami "Archdiocese of Miami") operates the area's Catholic private schools, which include [Immaculata-Lasalle High School](/wiki/Immaculata-Lasalle_High_School "Immaculata-Lasalle High School") (in the Miami city limits), [St. Theresa School](/wiki/St._Theresa_School_(Coral_Gables,_Florida) "St. Theresa School (Coral Gables, Florida)") (Coral Gables), [Monsignor Edward Pace High School](/wiki/Monsignor_Edward_Pace_High_School "Monsignor Edward Pace High School") (Miami Gardens), and [St. Brendan High School](/wiki/St._Brendan_High_School "St. Brendan High School") (in Westchester), among numerous other Catholic elementary and high schools.[[279]](#cite_note-290)[[280]](#cite_note-291)[[281]](#cite_note-292) [Archbishop Curley-Notre Dame High School](/wiki/Archbishop_Curley-Notre_Dame_High_School "Archbishop Curley-Notre Dame High School") was in the Miami city limits until its closure in 2016.[[282]](#cite_note-293)

Catholic preparatory schools operated by religious orders in the area are [Belen Jesuit Preparatory School](/wiki/Belen_Jesuit_Preparatory_School "Belen Jesuit Preparatory School") (Tamiami) and [Christopher Columbus High School](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus_High_School_(Miami-Dade_County,_Florida) "Christopher Columbus High School (Miami-Dade County, Florida)") (Westchester) for boys and [Carrollton School of the Sacred Heart](/wiki/Carrollton_School_of_the_Sacred_Heart "Carrollton School of the Sacred Heart") (Miami city limits) and [Our Lady of Lourdes Academy](/wiki/Our_Lady_of_Lourdes_Academy "Our Lady of Lourdes Academy") (Ponce-Davis) for girls.[[283]](#cite_note-294)[[284]](#cite_note-295)[[285]](#cite_note-296)[[286]](#cite_note-297)

Non-denominational private schools in Miami are [Ransom Everglades](/wiki/Ransom_Everglades_School "Ransom Everglades School"), [Gulliver Preparatory School](/wiki/Gulliver_Preparatory_School "Gulliver Preparatory School"), [The Cushman](/w/index.php?title=The_Cushman&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Cushman (page does not exist)") School, and [Miami Country Day School](/wiki/Miami_Country_Day_School "Miami Country Day School"). Other schools in the area include [Samuel Scheck Hillel Community Day School](/wiki/Samuel_Scheck_Hillel_Community_Day_School "Samuel Scheck Hillel Community Day School"), [Dade Christian School](/wiki/Dade_Christian_School "Dade Christian School"), [Dawere International High School](/w/index.php?title=Dawere_International_High_School&action=edit&redlink=1 "Dawere International High School (page does not exist)"),[[287]](#cite_note-298)[[288]](#cite_note-299) [Palmer Trinity School](/wiki/Palmer_Trinity_School "Palmer Trinity School"), [Westminster Christian School](/wiki/Westminster_Christian_School_(Florida) "Westminster Christian School (Florida)"), and [Riviera Schools](/wiki/Riviera_Schools "Riviera Schools").[[289]](#cite_note-300)[[290]](#cite_note-301)[[291]](#cite_note-302)[[292]](#cite_note-303)[[293]](#cite_note-304)[[294]](#cite_note-305)

### Supplementary education

The [Miami Hoshuko](/wiki/Miami_Hoshuko "Miami Hoshuko"), is a [part-time Japanese school](/wiki/Hoshuko "Hoshuko") for Japanese citizens and ethnic Japanese people in the area. Previously it was located on [Virginia Key](/wiki/Virginia_Key "Virginia Key"), at the Rosenstiel School of Marine and Atmospheric Science.[[295]](#cite_note-306) Currently the school holds classes in [Westchester](/wiki/Westchester,_Florida "Westchester, Florida") and has offices in [Doral](/wiki/Doral,_Florida "Doral, Florida").[[296]](#cite_note-Miamihoshu-307)

## Media

Main article: [Media in Miami](/wiki/Media_in_Miami "Media in Miami")

See also: [Film in Miami](/wiki/Film_in_Miami "Film in Miami"), [List of newspapers in Florida](/wiki/List_of_newspapers_in_Florida "List of newspapers in Florida"), [List of radio stations in Florida](/wiki/List_of_radio_stations_in_Florida "List of radio stations in Florida"), and [List of television stations in Florida](/wiki/List_of_television_stations_in_Florida "List of television stations in Florida")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Miami_Herald_building.jpg/250px-Miami_Herald_building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miami_Herald_building.jpg)

The former headquarters of *[The Miami Herald](/wiki/Miami_Herald "Miami Herald")* on [Biscayne Bay](/wiki/Biscayne_Bay "Biscayne Bay")

Miami has one of the largest television markets in the nation and the second largest in the state of Florida after [Tampa Bay](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_area "Tampa Bay area").[[297]](#cite_note-308) Miami has several major newspapers, the main and largest newspaper being *[The Miami Herald](/wiki/The_Miami_Herald "The Miami Herald")*. *[El Nuevo Herald](/wiki/El_Nuevo_Herald "El Nuevo Herald")* is the major and largest Spanish-language newspaper. *The Miami Herald* and *El Nuevo Herald* are Miami's and South Florida's main, major and largest newspapers. The papers left their longtime home in Downtown Miami in 2013. The newspapers are now headquartered at the former home of [U.S. Southern Command](/wiki/U.S._Southern_Command "U.S. Southern Command") in [Doral](/wiki/Doral,_Florida "Doral, Florida").[[298]](#cite_note-309)

Other major newspapers include *[Miami Today](/wiki/Miami_Today "Miami Today")*, headquartered in Brickell, *[Miami New Times](/wiki/Miami_New_Times "Miami New Times")*, headquartered in [Midtown](/wiki/Midtown_Miami "Midtown Miami"), *[Miami SunPost](/wiki/Miami_SunPost "Miami SunPost")*, *[South Florida Business Journal](/wiki/South_Florida_Business_Journal "South Florida Business Journal")*, and *[The Miami Times](/wiki/The_Miami_Times "The Miami Times")*. An additional Spanish-language newspaper, *[Diario Las Americas](/wiki/Diario_Las_Americas "Diario Las Americas")* also serves Miami. Student newspapers from the local universities include the [University of Miami](/wiki/University_of_Miami "University of Miami")'s *[The Miami Hurricane](/wiki/The_Miami_Hurricane "The Miami Hurricane")*, [Florida International University](/wiki/Florida_International_University "Florida International University")'s *[The Beacon](/wiki/The_Beacon_(Florida_International_University) "The Beacon (Florida International University)")*, [Miami-Dade College](/wiki/Miami-Dade_College "Miami-Dade College")'s *The Metropolis*, and [Barry University](/wiki/Barry_University "Barry University")'s *The Buccaneer*. Many neighborhoods and neighboring areas have their own local newspapers, such as the *Aventura News*, *Coral Gables Tribune*, *Biscayne Bay Tribune*, *Biscayne Times*, and the *Palmetto Bay News*.

A number of magazines circulate throughout the greater Miami area, including *[Miami Monthly](/wiki/Miami_Monthly "Miami Monthly")*, Southeast Florida's only city/regional, and *[Ocean Drive](/wiki/Ocean_Drive_(magazine) "Ocean Drive (magazine)")*, a hot-spot social scene glossy.

Miami is the headquarters and main production city of many of the world's largest television networks, record label companies, broadcasting companies and production facilities, such as [Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo"), [Univision](/wiki/Univision "Univision"), [Univision Communications](/wiki/Univision_Communications "Univision Communications"), [Mega TV](/wiki/Mega_TV_(American_TV_network) "Mega TV (American TV network)"), [Universal Music Latin Entertainment](/wiki/Universal_Music_Latin_Entertainment "Universal Music Latin Entertainment"), [RCTV International](/wiki/RCTV_International "RCTV International") and [Sunbeam Television](/wiki/Sunbeam_Television "Sunbeam Television"). In 2009, Univision announced plans to build a new production studio in Miami, dubbed Univision Studios. Univision Studios is headquartered in Miami, and will produce programming for all of Univision Communications' television networks.[[299]](#cite_note-310)

Miami is the twelfth largest radio market and the seventeenth largest television market in the United States.[[300]](#cite_note-311)[[301]](#cite_note-312) Television stations serving the Miami area include [WFOR](/wiki/WFOR-TV "WFOR-TV") 4 ([CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS") [O&O](/wiki/Owned-and-operated_station "Owned-and-operated station")), [WTVJ](/wiki/WTVJ "WTVJ") 6 ([NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") O&O), [WSVN](/wiki/WSVN "WSVN") 7 ([Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company"), with [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") on DT2 via [WDFL-LD](/wiki/WDFL-LD "WDFL-LD") 18), [WLTV](/wiki/WLTV "WLTV") 23 ([Univision](/wiki/Univision "Univision") O&O), [WBFS](/wiki/WBFS-TV "WBFS-TV") 33 ([The CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW")), [WPXM](/wiki/WPXM "WPXM") 35 ([Ion](/wiki/Ion_Media "Ion Media") O&O), [WHFT](/wiki/WHFT "WHFT") 45 ([TBN](/wiki/Trinity_Broadcasting_Network "Trinity Broadcasting Network") (O&O), [WSCV](/wiki/WSCV "WSCV") 51 ([Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo")) and [WAMI](/wiki/WAMI-TV "WAMI-TV") 69 ([UniMás](/wiki/UniM%C3%A1s "UniMás") O&O). [Independent stations](/wiki/Independent_station "Independent station") include [WPLG](/wiki/WPLG "WPLG") 10 and [WSFL](/wiki/WSFL-TV "WSFL-TV") 39, while [WPBT](/wiki/WPBT "WPBT") 2 and [WLRN](/wiki/WLRN-TV "WLRN-TV") 17 are member stations of [PBS](/wiki/PBS "PBS").

## Transportation

Main article: [Transportation in South Florida](/wiki/Transportation_in_South_Florida "Transportation in South Florida")

See also: [Brightline](/wiki/Brightline "Brightline") and [Tri-Rail](/wiki/Tri-Rail "Tri-Rail")

In the 2016 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey"), 72.3% of working city of Miami residents commuted by driving alone, 8.7% carpooled, 9% used public transportation, and 3.7% walked. About 1.8% used all other forms of transportation, including taxicab, motorcycle, and bicycle. About 4.5% of working city of Miami residents worked at home.[[302]](#cite_note-transittable-313) In 2015, 19.9% of city of Miami households were without a car, which decreased to 18.6% in 2016. The national average was 8.7 percent in 2016. Miami averaged 1.24 cars per household in 2016, compared to a national average of 1.8 per household.[[303]](#cite_note-314)

### Expressways and roads

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Venetian_Causeway_South_Beach.jpg/250px-Venetian_Causeway_South_Beach.jpg)](/wiki/File:Venetian_Causeway_South_Beach.jpg)

[Venetian Causeway](/wiki/Venetian_Causeway "Venetian Causeway") (left) and [MacArthur Causeway](/wiki/MacArthur_Causeway "MacArthur Causeway") (right) connect [Downtown](/wiki/Greater_Downtown_Miami "Greater Downtown Miami") and [South Beach](/wiki/South_Beach "South Beach").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/A306%2C_Skyline_at_twilight%2C_Miami%2C_Florida%2C_USA%2C_2010.JPG/250px-A306%2C_Skyline_at_twilight%2C_Miami%2C_Florida%2C_USA%2C_2010.JPG)](/wiki/File:A306,_Skyline_at_twilight,_Miami,_Florida,_USA,_2010.JPG)

[State Road 886](/wiki/Florida_State_Road_886 "Florida State Road 886"), also known as Port Boulevard, connects [Downtown Miami](/wiki/Greater_Downtown_Miami "Greater Downtown Miami") and [PortMiami](/wiki/PortMiami "PortMiami") over [Biscayne Bay](/wiki/Biscayne_Bay "Biscayne Bay").

Miami's road system is based along the numerical Miami grid where [Flagler Street](/wiki/Flagler_Street "Flagler Street") forms the east–west [baseline](/wiki/Baseline_(surveying) "Baseline (surveying)") and [Miami Avenue](/wiki/Miami_Avenue "Miami Avenue") forms the north–south [meridian](/wiki/Meridian_(geography) "Meridian (geography)"). The corner of Flagler Street and Miami Avenue is in the middle of Downtown in front of the Downtown [Macy's](/wiki/Macy%27s "Macy's") (formerly the [Burdine's](/wiki/Burdine%27s "Burdine's") headquarters). The Miami grid is primarily numerical so that, for example, all street addresses north of Flagler Street and west of Miami Avenue have "NW" in their address. Because its point of origin is in Downtown, which is close to the coast, the "NW" and "SW" quadrants are much larger than the "SE" and "NE" quadrants. Many roads, especially major ones, are also named (e.g., [Tamiami Trail/SW 8th St](/wiki/Tamiami_Trail "Tamiami Trail")), although, with exceptions, the number is in more common usage among locals.

With few exceptions, within this grid north–south roads are designated as Courts, Roads, Avenues or Places (often remembered by their acronym), while east–west roads are streets, Terraces, Drives or occasionally Ways. Major roads in each direction are located at one mile intervals. There are 16 [blocks](/wiki/City_block "City block") to each mile on north–south avenues, and 10 blocks to each mile on east–west streets. Major north–south avenues generally end in "7" – e.g., 17th, 27th, 37th/Douglas Aves., 57th/Red Rd., 67th/Ludlam, 87th/Galloway, etc., all the way west beyond 177th/Krome Avenue. One prominent exception is 42nd Avenue, LeJeune Road, located at the half-mile point instead.

Major east–west streets to the south of Downtown are multiples of 16, though the beginning point of this system is at SW 8th St, one half-mile south of Flagler ("zeroth") Street. Thus, major streets are at 8th St., 24th St./Coral Way, 40th St./Bird, 56th/Miller, 72nd/ Sunset, 88th/N. Kendall, 104th (originally S. Kendall), 120th/Montgomery, 136th/Howard, 152nd/Coral Reef, 168th/Richmond, 184th/Eureka, 200th/Quail Roost, 216th/Hainlin Mill, 232nd/Silver Palm, 248th/Coconut Palm, etc., well into the 300s. Within the grid, odd-numbered addresses are generally on the north or east side, and even-numbered addresses are on the south or west side.

All streets and avenues in [Miami-Dade County](/wiki/Miami-Dade_County "Miami-Dade County") follow the Miami grid, with a few exceptions, most notably in [Coral Gables](/wiki/Coral_Gables,_Florida "Coral Gables, Florida"), [Hialeah](/wiki/Hialeah "Hialeah"), Coconut Grove and [Miami Beach](/wiki/Miami_Beach,_Florida "Miami Beach, Florida"). One neighborhood, [The Roads](/wiki/The_Roads "The Roads"), is named as such because its streets run off the Miami grid at a 45-degree angle, and therefore are all named roads.

[Miami-Dade County](/wiki/Miami-Dade_County "Miami-Dade County") is served by four Interstate Highways ([I-75](/wiki/Interstate_75_(Florida) "Interstate 75 (Florida)"), [I-95](/wiki/Interstate_95_in_Florida "Interstate 95 in Florida"), [I-195](/wiki/Interstate_195_(Florida) "Interstate 195 (Florida)"), [I-395](/wiki/Interstate_395_(Florida) "Interstate 395 (Florida)")) and several U.S. Highways including [U.S. Route 1](/wiki/U.S._Route_1_(Florida) "U.S. Route 1 (Florida)"), [U.S. Route 27](/wiki/U.S._Route_27_(Florida) "U.S. Route 27 (Florida)"), [U.S. Route 41](/wiki/U.S._Route_41_(Florida) "U.S. Route 41 (Florida)"), and [U.S. Route 441](/wiki/U.S._Route_441_(Florida) "U.S. Route 441 (Florida)").

Some of the major [Florida State Roads](/wiki/Florida_State_Roads "Florida State Roads") (and their common names) serving Miami are:

* [SR 112](/wiki/State_Road_112_(Florida) "State Road 112 (Florida)") (Airport Expressway): [Interstate 95](/wiki/Interstate_95_in_Florida "Interstate 95 in Florida") to [MIA](/wiki/Miami_International_Airport "Miami International Airport")
* [Homestead Extension of Florida's Turnpike](/wiki/Homestead_Extension_of_Florida%27s_Turnpike "Homestead Extension of Florida's Turnpike") (SR 821): [Florida's Turnpike](/wiki/Florida%27s_Turnpike "Florida's Turnpike") mainline (SR 91)/[Miami Gardens](/wiki/Miami_Gardens,_Florida "Miami Gardens, Florida") to [U.S. Route 1](/wiki/U.S._Route_1_(Florida) "U.S. Route 1 (Florida)")/[Florida City](/wiki/Florida_City,_Florida "Florida City, Florida")
* [SR 826](/wiki/State_Road_826_(Florida) "State Road 826 (Florida)") (Palmetto Expressway): [Golden Glades Interchange](/wiki/Golden_Glades_Interchange "Golden Glades Interchange") to U.S. Route 1/[Pinecrest](/wiki/Pinecrest,_Florida "Pinecrest, Florida")
* [SR 836](/wiki/State_Road_836_(Florida) "State Road 836 (Florida)") (Dolphin Expressway): Downtown to SW 137th Ave via [MIA](/wiki/Miami_International_Airport "Miami International Airport")
* [SR 874](/wiki/State_Road_874_(Florida) "State Road 874 (Florida)") (Don Shula Expressway): 826/Bird Road to Homestead Extension of Florida's Turnpike/[Kendall](/wiki/Kendall,_Florida "Kendall, Florida")
* [SR 878](/wiki/State_Road_878_(Florida) "State Road 878 (Florida)") (Snapper Creek Expressway): SR 874/[Kendall](/wiki/Kendall,_Florida "Kendall, Florida") to U.S. Route 1/Pinecrest & [South Miami](/wiki/South_Miami,_Florida "South Miami, Florida")
* [SR 924](/wiki/State_Road_924_(Florida) "State Road 924 (Florida)") (Gratigny Parkway) [Miami Lakes](/wiki/Miami_Lakes,_Florida "Miami Lakes, Florida") to [Opa-locka](/wiki/Opa-locka,_Florida "Opa-locka, Florida")

Miami has six major [causeways](/wiki/Causeway "Causeway") that span over Biscayne Bay connecting the western mainland, with the eastern barrier islands along the Atlantic Ocean. The [Rickenbacker Causeway](/wiki/Rickenbacker_Causeway "Rickenbacker Causeway") is the southernmost causeway and connects Brickell to [Virginia Key](/wiki/Virginia_Key "Virginia Key") and [Key Biscayne](/wiki/Key_Biscayne,_Florida "Key Biscayne, Florida"). The [Venetian Causeway](/wiki/Venetian_Causeway "Venetian Causeway") and [MacArthur Causeway](/wiki/MacArthur_Causeway "MacArthur Causeway") connect [Downtown](/wiki/Greater_Downtown_Miami "Greater Downtown Miami") with [South Beach](/wiki/South_Beach "South Beach"). The [Julia Tuttle Causeway](/wiki/Julia_Tuttle_Causeway "Julia Tuttle Causeway") connects [Midtown](/wiki/Midtown_Miami "Midtown Miami") and [Miami Beach](/wiki/Miami_Beach "Miami Beach"). The [79th Street Causeway](/wiki/Florida_State_Road_934 "Florida State Road 934") connects the [Upper East Side](/wiki/Upper_Eastside,_Miami,_Florida "Upper Eastside, Miami, Florida") with [North Beach](/wiki/North_Beach_(Miami_Beach) "North Beach (Miami Beach)"). The northernmost causeway, the [Broad Causeway](/wiki/Broad_Causeway "Broad Causeway"), is the smallest of Miami's six causeways and connects [North Miami](/wiki/North_Miami,_Florida "North Miami, Florida") to [Bay Harbor Islands](/wiki/Bay_Harbor_Islands "Bay Harbor Islands") and [Bal Harbour](/wiki/Bal_Harbour,_Florida "Bal Harbour, Florida").

In 2007, Miami was identified as having the rudest drivers in the United States, the second year in a row to have been cited, in a poll commissioned by automobile club AutoVantage.[[304]](#cite_note-315) Miami is also consistently ranked as one of the most dangerous cities in the United States for pedestrians.[[305]](#cite_note-316)

### Public transportation

See also: [Metrobus (Miami-Dade County)](/wiki/Metrobus_(Miami-Dade_County) "Metrobus (Miami-Dade County)"), [Metromover](/wiki/Metromover "Metromover"), and [Miami-Dade Transit](/wiki/Miami-Dade_Transit "Miami-Dade Transit")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Miami_Metrorail_Hitachi_train_20190117.jpg/250px-Miami_Metrorail_Hitachi_train_20190117.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miami_Metrorail_Hitachi_train_20190117.jpg)

Miami's [Metrorail](/wiki/Metrorail_(Miami-Dade_County) "Metrorail (Miami-Dade County)") is the city's [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system and connects Miami with its outlying suburbs.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Tri_Rail_616_Opa-locka_Station_%288439701464%29.jpg/250px-Tri_Rail_616_Opa-locka_Station_%288439701464%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tri_Rail_616_Opa-locka_Station_(8439701464).jpg)

[Tri-Rail](/wiki/Tri-Rail "Tri-Rail") is Miami's [commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") that runs north–south from Miami's suburbs in [West Palm Beach](/wiki/West_Palm_Beach,_Florida "West Palm Beach, Florida") to [Miami International Airport](/wiki/Miami_International_Airport "Miami International Airport").

[Public transportation](/wiki/Public_transportation "Public transportation") in Miami is operated by [Miami-Dade Transit](/wiki/Miami-Dade_Transit "Miami-Dade Transit") and [SFRTA](/wiki/South_Florida_Regional_Transportation_Authority "South Florida Regional Transportation Authority"), and includes [commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") ([Tri-Rail](/wiki/Tri-Rail "Tri-Rail")), heavy-rail [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") ([Metrorail](/wiki/Metrorail_(Miami-Dade_County) "Metrorail (Miami-Dade County)")), an elevated [people mover](/wiki/People_mover "People mover") ([Metromover](/wiki/Metromover "Metromover")), and buses ([Metrobus](/wiki/Miami-Dade_Transit#Metrobus "Miami-Dade Transit")). Miami has Florida's highest transit ridership as about 17% of Miamians use transit on a daily basis.[[306]](#cite_note-317) The average Miami public transit commute on weekdays is 90 minutes, while 39% of public transit riders commute for more than 2 hours a day. The average wait time at a public transit stop or station is 18 minutes, while 37% of riders wait for more than 20 minutes on average every day. The average single trip distance with public transit is 7.46 mi (12 km), while 38% travel more than 8.08 mi (13 km) in each direction.[[307]](#cite_note-318)

Miami's heavy-rail rapid transit system, [Metrorail](/wiki/Metrorail_(Miami-Dade_County) "Metrorail (Miami-Dade County)"), is an elevated system comprising two lines and 23 stations on a 24.4-mile (39.3 km)-long line. Metrorail connects the urban western suburbs of [Hialeah](/wiki/Hialeah,_Florida "Hialeah, Florida"), [Medley](/wiki/Medley,_Florida "Medley, Florida"), and inner-city Miami with suburban [The Roads](/wiki/The_Roads "The Roads"), [Coconut Grove](/wiki/Coconut_Grove "Coconut Grove"), [Coral Gables](/wiki/Coral_Gables,_Florida "Coral Gables, Florida"), [South Miami](/wiki/South_Miami "South Miami"), and urban [Kendall](/wiki/Kendall,_Florida "Kendall, Florida") via the central business districts of [Miami International Airport](/wiki/Miami_International_Airport "Miami International Airport"), the [Health District](/wiki/Health_District_(Miami) "Health District (Miami)"), and Downtown. A free, elevated [people mover](/wiki/People_mover "People mover"), [Metromover](/wiki/Metromover "Metromover"), operates 21 stations on three different lines in greater Downtown Miami, with a station at roughly every two blocks of Downtown and Brickell. Several expansion projects are being funded by a transit development sales tax surcharge throughout Miami-Dade County.[[308]](#cite_note-319)

[Tri-Rail](/wiki/Tri-Rail "Tri-Rail"), a commuter rail system operated by the [South Florida Regional Transportation Authority](/wiki/South_Florida_Regional_Transportation_Authority "South Florida Regional Transportation Authority") (SFRTA), runs from [Miami International Airport](/wiki/Miami_International_Airport "Miami International Airport") northward to [West Palm Beach](/wiki/West_Palm_Beach,_Florida "West Palm Beach, Florida"), making eighteen stops throughout [Miami-Dade](/wiki/Miami-Dade_County "Miami-Dade County"), [Broward](/wiki/Broward_County "Broward County"), and [Palm Beach](/wiki/Palm_Beach_County "Palm Beach County") counties.[[309]](#cite_note-320)

The [Miami Intermodal Center](/wiki/Miami_Intermodal_Center "Miami Intermodal Center") is a massive transportation hub servicing [Metrorail](/wiki/Metrorail_(Miami-Dade_County) "Metrorail (Miami-Dade County)"), [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak"), [Tri-Rail](/wiki/Tri-Rail "Tri-Rail"), [Brightline](/wiki/Brightline "Brightline"), [Metrobus](/wiki/Miami-Dade_Transit#Metrobus "Miami-Dade Transit"), [Greyhound Lines](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines"), [taxis](/wiki/Taxicab "Taxicab"), [rental cars](/wiki/Rental_cars "Rental cars"), [MIA Mover](/wiki/MIA_Mover "MIA Mover"), private automobiles, bicycles and pedestrians adjacent to Miami International Airport. Miami Intermodal Center was completed in 2010, and is serving about 150,000 commuters and travelers in the Miami area. Phase I of [MiamiCentral](/wiki/MiamiCentral "MiamiCentral") Station was completed in 2012, and the Tri-Rail part of Phase II was completed in 2015, but the construction of the Amtrak part remains delayed.

Two new light rail systems, Baylink and the Miami Streetcar, have been proposed and are currently in the planning stage. BayLink would connect Downtown with [South Beach](/wiki/South_Beach "South Beach"), and the Miami Streetcar would connect Downtown with [Midtown](/wiki/Midtown_Miami "Midtown Miami").

Miami is the southern terminus of [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak")'s Atlantic Coast services, running two lines, the [Silver Meteor](/wiki/Silver_Meteor "Silver Meteor") and the [Silver Star](/wiki/Silver_Star_(Amtrak_train) "Silver Star (Amtrak train)"), both terminating in New York City. The Miami Amtrak Station is located in the suburb of [Hialeah](/wiki/Hialeah "Hialeah") near the [Tri-Rail/Metrorail Station](/wiki/Tri-Rail_and_Metrorail_transfer_station "Tri-Rail and Metrorail transfer station") on NW 79 St and NW 38 Ave. Current construction of the [MiamiCentral](/wiki/MiamiCentral "MiamiCentral") Station will move all Amtrak operations from its current out-of-the-way location to a centralized location with [Metrorail](/wiki/Miami_Metrorail "Miami Metrorail"), [MIA Mover](/wiki/MIA_Mover "MIA Mover"), [Tri-Rail](/wiki/Tri-Rail "Tri-Rail"), [Miami International Airport](/wiki/Miami_International_Airport "Miami International Airport"), and the [Miami Intermodal Center](/wiki/Miami_Intermodal_Center "Miami Intermodal Center") all within the same station closer to Downtown. The station was expected to be completed by 2012,[[310]](#cite_note-321) but experienced several delays and was later expected to be completed in late 2014,[[311]](#cite_note-322) again pushed back to early 2015.[[312]](#cite_note-MICjanuary-323)

### Airports

See also: [Miami International Airport](/wiki/Miami_International_Airport "Miami International Airport"), [Fort Lauderdale-Hollywood International Airport](/wiki/Fort_Lauderdale-Hollywood_International_Airport "Fort Lauderdale-Hollywood International Airport"), and [Palm Beach International Airport](/wiki/Palm_Beach_International_Airport "Palm Beach International Airport")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Miami_International_Airport_%28KMIA-MIA%29_%288204606870%29.jpg/250px-Miami_International_Airport_%28KMIA-MIA%29_%288204606870%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Miami_International_Airport_(KMIA-MIA)_(8204606870).jpg)

[Miami International Airport](/wiki/Miami_International_Airport "Miami International Airport"), the nation's 10th-largest airport

[Miami International Airport](/wiki/Miami_International_Airport "Miami International Airport") serves as the primary international airport of the Greater Miami Area. One of the busiest international airports in the world because of its centric location, Miami International Airport caters to over 45 million passengers a year. The airport is a major hub and the largest international gateway for [American Airlines](/wiki/American_Airlines "American Airlines").[[313]](#cite_note-miami-airport.com-324)

Miami International is the second busiest airport by passenger traffic in Florida, the United States' third-largest international port of entry for foreign air passengers, behind New York's [John F. Kennedy International Airport](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_International_Airport "John F. Kennedy International Airport") and [Los Angeles International Airport](/wiki/Los_Angeles_International_Airport "Los Angeles International Airport"). The airport's extensive international route network includes non-stop flights to over seventy international cities in North and [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"), [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), and the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East").[[313]](#cite_note-miami-airport.com-324)

Nearby [Fort Lauderdale–Hollywood International Airport](/wiki/Fort_Lauderdale%E2%80%93Hollywood_International_Airport "Fort Lauderdale–Hollywood International Airport") and [Palm Beach International Airport](/wiki/Palm_Beach_International_Airport "Palm Beach International Airport") also serve commercial traffic in the Miami area.[[314]](#cite_note-325) [Miami-Opa Locka Executive Airport](/wiki/Miami-Opa_Locka_Executive_Airport "Miami-Opa Locka Executive Airport") in [Opa-locka](/wiki/Opa-locka,_Florida "Opa-locka, Florida") and [Miami Executive Airport](/wiki/Miami_Executive_Airport "Miami Executive Airport") in an unincorporated area southwest of Miami serve general aviation traffic in the Miami area.

### Cycling and walking

The city government under former mayor [Manny Diaz](/wiki/Manny_Diaz_(Florida_politician) "Manny Diaz (Florida politician)") took an ambitious stance in support of [bicycling](/wiki/Bicycling "Bicycling") in Miami for both recreation and commuting.[[315]](#cite_note-326) In 2010, Miami was ranked as the 44th-most bike-friendly city in the U.S., according to *[Bicycling Magazine](/wiki/Bicycling_Magazine "Bicycling Magazine")*.[[316]](#cite_note-327)

A 2011 study by [Walk Score](/wiki/Walk_Score "Walk Score") ranked Miami the eighth-most walkable of the 50 largest cities in the U.S.[[317]](#cite_note-328)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** Bahamians were farming along the Miami River before 1830. Richard Fitzpatrick established a plantation there in 1830, but abandoned it when the [Second Seminole War](/wiki/Second_Seminole_War "Second Seminole War") (1835–1843) began. The U.S. Army established [Fort Dallas](/wiki/Fort_Dallas "Fort Dallas") there in 1836, but left the fort in 1841. William English reopened Fitzpatrick's plantation after the war and sold city lots, but left the area at the end of the 1840s. The Army returned to the fort in 1849–1851, and again for the Third Seminole War (1855–1858).[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)
